entry.title: Re: SAC Bug: Change in Numeric point widget display is SA...
entry.content:
Hello @konakalp 

This behavior is most likely due to a recent SAC update or formatting change in the Numeric Point widget. When a measure is defined as a currency measure, SAC now displays the currency code (for example, CHF) for both the actual value and the variance.

 

Thanks,
Sravan

entry.title: Re: Unit Conversion in SAP Datasphere using T006 (LB to KG Example)
entry.content: Both approaches ultimately achieve the same conversion result. However, the SAP-delivered Unit Conversion Views provide some additional conveniences, such as standard value-help views. These allow users to select units from a dropdown list with meaningful descriptions (for example, "Kilogram" instead of just technical unit codes like "KG"). When target units are exposed through input parameters, these value-help views improve the user experience by providing a standardized and business-friendly selection list.

entry.title: Warehouse Task Instead of Posting Change for stock type UU in EWM Inspection
entry.content:
Hi Gurus,

I have a question regarding the standard behavior of EWM Inspection.

As far as I know, in the standard SAP configuration under:

SPRO → SAP Customizing Implementation Guide → SCM Extended Warehouse Management → Extended Warehouse Management → Cross-Process Settings → Quality Management → Inspection Results → Maintain Follow-Up Action → Assign Logistical Follow-Up Actions

when assigning the internal follow-up action, we have to select "PUT AWAY".

With this standard configuration, after the Usage Decision, the system creates a Posting Change that changes the stock type from Quality Inspection Stock to Unrestricted-Use Warehouse Stock. This Posting Change is automatically confirmed, so no manual confirmation is required.

However, my business requirement is different.

Instead of generating an automatically confirmed Posting Change, I would like the system to generate a Warehouse Task with the same source and destination information as the Posting Change, but the Warehouse Task should remain open and require manual confirmation.

I have already tried maintaining a Warehouse Process Type (WPT) in the follow-up action configuration (see the screenshot below). However, when the internal follow-up action is set to PUT AWAY, SAP seems to ignore the configured WPT and still generates an automatically confirmed Posting Change.

 

My questions are:

  1. Is this the standard SAP behavior?
  2. Is it possible through standard configuration to generate a Warehouse Task instead of an auto-confirmed Posting Change after EWM Inspection?
  3. If this is not supported by standard SAP, is there any recommended BAdI, enhancement, or customer exit that can be used to achieve this requirement?

Any advice or experience would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance!

#EWM

entry.title: Re: Zertifizierung „SAP Certified – Data Analyst – SAP An...
entry.content:
Hallo @Carine-Kuimi90_ Carine,

vielen Dank für deinen Beitrag und viel Erfolg bei der Vorbereitung auf die Zertifizierung "SAP Certified – Data Analyst – SAP Analytics Cloud". Es ist großartig zu sehen, dass du bereits mehrere Einheiten des Learning Journeys abgeschlossen hast. 

Ich sehe, dass du dieselbe Frage bereits in den Data & Analytics Learning Group Discussions gepostet hast. Da es dort speziell um den Austausch mit anderen Lernenden, Prüfungsvorbereitung, Erfahrungen und Lerntipps geht, würde ich empfehlen, die Diskussion dort fortzuführen.

Um die Informationen für alle Beteiligten an einer Stelle zu bündeln und doppelte Diskussionen zu vermeiden, schließe ich diesen Thread. Bitte nutze für weitere Fragen und den Austausch den bestehenden Beitrag in der Data & Analytics Learning Group.

To enable all community members to follow the discussion, here is the English translation of this response:

I can see that you have already posted the same question in the Data & Analytics Learning Group Discussions. Since that group is specifically intended for connecting with fellow learners, sharing certification experiences, preparation tips, and learning resources, I recommend continuing the discussion there.

To keep all information in one place and avoid duplicate conversations, this thread will be closed. Please continue the discussion and any follow-up questions in your existing Data & Analytics Learning Group post.

 

Vielen Dank für dein Verständnis und viel Erfolg bei deiner Zertifizierungsvorbereitung!

Viele Grüße
Margit

entry.title: Re: Create Model
entry.content:
hi @Samar_Alareqi ,

The Create button is disabled because a File Server Connection has not been selected. Before creating a model from a file, make sure a valid file server connection is available and selected in the dropdown. If the list is empty, it usually means that no file server connection has been configured or your user account does not have the required authorization to access it. If you're using a newer version of SAP Analytics Cloud, you can also create a model by uploading an Excel or CSV file directly without configuring a file server connection. If the issue persists, ask your SAC administrator to verify your permissions and the available data connections

entry.title: Injecting Live KPI Tiles into a Fiori Elements V2 List Report Page
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

Introduction

The SAP Fiori Elements V2 List Report is a standard, highly efficient floorplan for displaying tabular data with built-in filtering, sorting, and object page navigation. However, a common business requirement is to display high-level analytical metrics — a dashboard-style KPI tile area — directly above the SmartFilterBar. Out of the box, Fiori Elements V2 does not provide a native configuration for this.

This guide covers a complete, working implementation to programmatically inject three live KPI tiles into a Fiori Elements V2 List Report header using the following:

  • sap.suite.ui.generic.template (V2 Smart Templates)
  • Northwind OData V2 service via a local proxy
  • sap.m.GenericTile with NumericContent
  • V2 Controller Extension (plain-object pattern) for safe Control Tree injection and data fetching

 

Why I Wrote This

Recently, I was tasked with adding a "dashboard" feel to a standard Fiori Elements List Report. The moment I started researching the best way to implement KPI tiles above the filter bar, I realized there wasn't a clear, end-to-end guide for V2 Smart Templates.

Most documentation either pointed towards V4 ControllerExtension patterns (which crash V2 apps), relied on Analytical List Pages (which is a different floorplan entirely), or suggested using the SmartFilterBarControlConfigurationExtension (which breaks the UI layout).

This post walks through the complete, robust flow: extending the V2 controller, safely injecting the tiles into the UI5 Control Tree (without touching the DOM), fetching aggregate counts efficiently using the standard OData model, and linking those tiles to custom interactive fragments.

 

First, Let's Understand the Concept—The Factory Car

Before jumping into code, here is how I approach customizing Fiori Elements.

Picture a Fiori Elements app as a factory-built car. It comes pre-assembled, highly optimized, and runs perfectly. If we want to add a custom hood ornament (our KPI tiles), we can't just bolt it on anywhere, it will damage the engine (the framework).

Instead of trying to force standard XML view extensions into slots that aren't built for layouts, we use JavaScript to gently place our tiles into the DynamicPageHeader right after the view renders. This keeps the factory engine untouched while giving the users the custom dashboard they want.

 

Application Overview

The setup relies on standard SAPUI5 controls injected cleanly into the generated view. Here is the folder structure I used:

project-root/
│
├── webapp/
│   ├── annotations/
│   │   └── annotation.xml
│   ├── ext/
│   │   ├── controller/
│   │   │   └── CustomTilesController.controller.js   ← Tile injection & logic
│   │   └── fragment/
│   │       ├── CustomersDialog.fragment.xml          ← Customers popup
│   │       └── ProductsDialog.fragment.xml           ← Products popup
│   ├── i18n/
│   ├── localService/mainService/
│   │   └── metadata.xml
│   ├── Component.js
│   ├── index.html
│   └── manifest.json

 

Step 1: Configuring manifest.json

To inject our custom logic, we only need a single controller extension entry. We do not need an sap.ui.viewExtensions entry because we will inject the tiles programmatically.

Add the following to the sap.ui5 -> extends -> extensions block in manifest.json:

"sap.ui5": {
  "extends": {
    "extensions": {
      "sap.ui.controllerExtensions": {
        "sap.suite.ui.generic.template.ListReport.view.ListReport": {
          "controllerName": "tilesinlistreport.ext.controller.CustomTilesController"
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

 

Step 2: The Controller Extension (Injection & Logic)

Create a controller file at webapp/ext/controller/CustomTilesController.controller.js .

This is the core of the solution. It builds the tiles, waits for the standard Fiori Elements view to render, finds the DynamicPageHeader, and injects our layout. Importantly, it uses the standard OData V2 model already attached to the view to fetch aggregate data, avoiding CORS issues and native fetch() errors.

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/m/GenericTile",
    "sap/m/TileContent",
    "sap/m/NumericContent",
    "sap/ui/layout/HorizontalLayout",
    "sap/ui/model/Filter",
    "sap/ui/model/FilterOperator",
    "sap/m/MessageToast",
    "sap/ui/core/Fragment"
], function (GenericTile, TileContent, NumericContent, HorizontalLayout,
             Filter, FilterOperator, MessageToast, Fragment) {
    "use strict";

    return {
        // Called by V2 Smart Template after the view renders
        onAfterRendering: function () {
            if (!this._bTilesInjected) {
                this._bTilesInjected = true;
                this._injectTiles();
            }
        },

        // Build and inject tile layout into the DynamicPageHeader
        _injectTiles: function () {
            var oView = this.getView();

            // Store NumericContent refs for value updates after data fetch
            this._oNumTotalOrders = new NumericContent({ value: "...", valueColor: "Good",    indicator: "None", withMargin: false });
            this._oNumProducts    = new NumericContent({ value: "...", valueColor: "Neutral", indicator: "None", withMargin: false });
            this._oNumCustomers   = new NumericContent({ value: "...", valueColor: "Neutral", indicator: "None", withMargin: false });

            var oLayout = new HorizontalLayout({
                allowWrapping: true,
                content: [
                    this._makeTile("Total Orders",    "All records",  "Orders",    this._oNumTotalOrders, this._onTotalOrdersPress.bind(this)),
                    this._makeTile("Total Products",  "In catalogue", "Products",  this._oNumProducts,    this._onProductsPress.bind(this)),
                    this._makeTile("Total Customers", "Registered",   "Customers", this._oNumCustomers,   this._onCustomersPress.bind(this))
                ]
            }).addStyleClass("sapUiSmallMarginTop sapUiSmallMarginBottom sapUiSmallMarginBeginEnd");

            // Primary strategy: Inject into DynamicPageHeader
            var aAll = oView.findAggregatedObjects(true);
            var oDynamicPage = null;
            var oSFB = null;

            for (var i = 0; i < aAll.length; i++) {
                var sName = aAll[i].getMetadata().getName();
                if (sName === "sap.f.DynamicPage") { oDynamicPage = aAll[i]; }
                if (sName === "sap.ui.comp.smartfilterbar.SmartFilterBar") { oSFB = aAll[i]; }
            }

            if (oDynamicPage && oDynamicPage.getHeader()) {
                oDynamicPage.getHeader().addContent(oLayout);
                this._fetchAllTileData();
                return;
            }

            // Fallback strategy: Inject via UI5 Control Tree (NO DOM MANIPULATION)
            if (oSFB) {
                var oParent = oSFB.getParent();
                if (oParent && typeof oParent.insertContent === "function") {
                    oParent.insertContent(oLayout, 0); // Insert above the SmartFilterBar
                    this._fetchAllTileData();
                } else if (oParent && typeof oParent.insertItem === "function") {
                    oParent.insertItem(oLayout, 0);
                    this._fetchAllTileData();
                }
            }
        },

        // GenericTile factory — MUST use tileContent aggregation in constructor
        _makeTile: function (sHeader, sSubheader, sUnit, oNumericContent, fnPress) {
            var oTile = new GenericTile({
                header: sHeader,
                subheader: sSubheader,
                frameType: "TwoByOne",
                tileContent: [
                    new TileContent({ unit: sUnit, content: oNumericContent })
                ]
            });
            oTile.addStyleClass("sapUiTinyMarginEnd");
            if (fnPress) { oTile.attachPress(fnPress); }
            return oTile;
        },

        // ── Press Handlers ────────────────────────────────────────────────

        _onTotalOrdersPress: function () {
            var oSFB = this._getSmartFilterBar();
            if (oSFB) { oSFB.clear(); oSFB.search(); }
            var sCount = this._oNumTotalOrders.getValue();
            MessageToast.show("Showing all " + sCount + " orders");
        },

        _onProductsPress: function () {
            var oView = this.getView();
            var sTitle = "Products (" + this._oNumProducts.getValue() + ")";
            if (!this._oProductsDialog) {
                Fragment.load({
                    id: oView.getId() + "-products",
                    name: "tilesinlistreport.ext.fragment.ProductsDialog",
                    controller: this
                }).then(function (oDialog) {
                    this._oProductsDialog = oDialog;
                    oView.addDependent(oDialog);
                    oDialog.setTitle(sTitle);
                    oDialog.open();
                }.bind(this));
            } else {
                this._oProductsDialog.setTitle(sTitle);
                this._oProductsDialog.open();
            }
        },

        onProductsDialogClose: function () {
            if (this._oProductsDialog) { this._oProductsDialog.close(); }
        },

        onProductSearch: function (oEvent) {
            var sQuery = oEvent.getParameter("newValue") || oEvent.getParameter("query") || "";
            var oTable = sap.ui.core.Fragment.byId(this.getView().getId() + "-products", "productsTable");
            if (oTable) {
                var oBinding = oTable.getBinding("items");
                oBinding.filter(sQuery ? [new Filter([
                    new Filter("ProductName", FilterOperator.Contains, sQuery)
                ], false)] : []);
            }
        },

        _onCustomersPress: function () {
            var oView = this.getView();
            var sTitle = "Customers (" + this._oNumCustomers.getValue() + ")";
            if (!this._oCustomersDialog) {
                Fragment.load({
                    id: oView.getId() + "-customers",
                    name: "tilesinlistreport.ext.fragment.CustomersDialog",
                    controller: this
                }).then(function (oDialog) {
                    this._oCustomersDialog = oDialog;
                    oView.addDependent(oDialog);
                    oDialog.setTitle(sTitle);
                    oDialog.open();
                }.bind(this));
            } else {
                this._oCustomersDialog.setTitle(sTitle);
                this._oCustomersDialog.open();
            }
        },

        onCustomersDialogClose: function () {
            if (this._oCustomersDialog) { this._oCustomersDialog.close(); }
        },

        onCustomerSearch: function (oEvent) {
            var sQuery = oEvent.getParameter("newValue") || oEvent.getParameter("query") || "";
            var oTable = sap.ui.core.Fragment.byId(this.getView().getId() + "-customers", "customersTable");
            if (oTable) {
                var oBinding = oTable.getBinding("items");
                oBinding.filter(sQuery ? [new Filter([
                    new Filter("CompanyName",  FilterOperator.Contains, sQuery),
                    new Filter("ContactName",  FilterOperator.Contains, sQuery),
                    new Filter("CustomerID",   FilterOperator.Contains, sQuery),
                    new Filter("City",         FilterOperator.Contains, sQuery),
                    new Filter("Country",      FilterOperator.Contains, sQuery)
                ], false)] : []);
            }
        },

        // ── Helpers & Data Fetching ───────────────────────────────────────

        _getSmartFilterBar: function () {
            var aAll = this.getView().findAggregatedObjects(true);
            for (var i = 0; i < aAll.length; i++) {
                if (aAll[i].getMetadata().getName() === "sap.ui.comp.smartfilterbar.SmartFilterBar")
                    return aAll[i];
            }
            return null;
        },

        _fetchAllTileData: function () {
            // Using the existing standard OData V2 Model attached to the view.
            var oModel = this.getView().getModel();
            var that = this;

            var fnGetCount = function (sPath) {
                return new Promise(function (resolve) {
                    oModel.read(sPath, {
                        urlParameters: { "$top": 0, "$inlinecount": "allpages" },
                        success: function (oData) {
                            var iCount = (oData && oData.__count) ? parseInt(oData.__count, 10) : 0;
                            resolve(iCount);
                        },
                        error: function () {
                            resolve(0); // Fail gracefully
                        }
                    });
                });
            };

            Promise.all([
                fnGetCount("/Orders"),
                fnGetCount("/Products"),
                fnGetCount("/Customers")
            ]).then(function (aCounts) {
                that._oNumTotalOrders.setValue(String(aCounts[0]));
                that._oNumProducts.setValue(String(aCounts[1]));
                that._oNumCustomers.setValue(String(aCounts[2]));
            });
        }
    };
});

 

Step 3: Interactive Dialog Fragments

When a user interacts with the "Total Customers" or "Total Products" tiles, a searchable dialog opens. These are built using standard XML fragments binding directly to the existing OData model.

Note that the dialog titles in the fragments below are left as plain labels ("Products", "Customers") rather than hardcoded counts—the controller sets the live count on the title at runtime (see _onProductsPress / _onCustomersPress in Step 2), so the number a user sees always matches the tile above it.

Here is the setup for the Products Dialog (webapp/ext/fragment/ProductsDialog.fragment.xml)

<core:FragmentDefinition xmlns="sap.m" xmlns:core="sap.ui.core">
    <Dialog id="productsDialog" title="Products" contentWidth="70%" contentHeight="60%" resizable="true" draggable="true">
        <content>
            <Table id="productsTable" items="{/Products}" growing="true" growingThreshold="20">
                <headerToolbar>
                    <Toolbar>
                        <Title text="All Products"/>
                        <ToolbarSpacer/>
                        <SearchField width="30%" search=".onProductSearch" liveChange=".onProductSearch"/>
                    </Toolbar>
                </headerToolbar>
                <columns>
                    <Column width="5rem"><Text text="ID"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Product Name"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Unit Price"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Units In Stock"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Units On Order"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Discontinued"/></Column>
                </columns>
                <items>
                    <ColumnListItem>
                        <cells>
                            <Text text="{ProductID}"/>
                            <Text text="{ProductName}"/>
                            <ObjectNumber number="{UnitPrice}" unit="USD"/>
                            <Text text="{UnitsInStock}"/>
                            <Text text="{UnitsOnOrder}"/>
                            <Text text="{Discontinued}"/>
                        </cells>
                    </ColumnListItem>
                </items>
            </Table>
        </content>
        <beginButton>
            <Button text="Close" press=".onProductsDialogClose"/>
        </beginButton>
    </Dialog>
</core:FragmentDefinition>

 

Here is the setup for the Customers Dialog (webapp/ext/fragment/CustomersDialog.fragment.xml)

<core:FragmentDefinition xmlns="sap.m" xmlns:core="sap.ui.core">
    <Dialog id="customersDialog" title="Customers" contentWidth="70%" contentHeight="60%" resizable="true" draggable="true">
        <content>
            <Table id="customersTable" items="{/Customers}" growing="true" growingThreshold="20">
                <headerToolbar>
                    <Toolbar>
                        <Title text="All Registered Customers"/>
                        <ToolbarSpacer/>
                        <SearchField width="30%" search=".onCustomerSearch" liveChange=".onCustomerSearch"/>
                    </Toolbar>
                </headerToolbar>
                <columns>
                    <Column width="8rem"><Text text="Customer ID"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Company Name"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Contact Name"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Contact Title"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="City"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Country"/></Column>
                    <Column><Text text="Phone"/></Column>
                </columns>
                <items>
                    <ColumnListItem>
                        <cells>
                            <Text text="{CustomerID}"/>
                            <Text text="{CompanyName}"/>
                            <Text text="{ContactName}"/>
                            <Text text="{ContactTitle}"/>
                            <Text text="{City}"/>
                            <Text text="{Country}"/>
                            <Text text="{Phone}"/>
                        </cells>
                    </ColumnListItem>
                </items>
            </Table>
        </content>
        <beginButton>
            <Button text="Close" press=".onCustomersDialogClose"/>
        </beginButton>
    </Dialog>
</core:FragmentDefinition>

 

A Few Things to Keep in Mind

When customizing standard templates, it is incredibly easy to run into framework errors. Here are the pitfalls to watch out for:

  1. Avoid direct DOM manipulation — Never use document.createElement or parentNode.insertBefore in Fiori Elements. If the view re-renders, our DOM elements will be destroyed. Always use framework aggregations like .insertContent() or .insertItem() to safely place elements into the UI5 Control Tree.
  2. Leverage the OData model for counts — Avoid hardcoded URLs and native fetch() calls. Use oModel.read() with the URL parameters "$top": 0 and "$inlinecount": "allpages". This fetches just the count without downloading a heavy payload, and it natively handles SAP Cloud routing, BTP destinations, and security headers.
  3. Wrong extension point — Do NOT use SmartFilterBarControlConfigurationExtension for layout tiles. That slot strictly accepts sap.ui.comp.smartfilterbar.ControlConfiguration elements. If we attempt to place a layout container there, the app will throw an aggregation error.
  4. Controller pattern mismatch—V2 extensions mandate a plain object return pattern (return { ... };). If we accidentally use the V4 ControllerExtension.extend() pattern, our V2 app will crash immediately upon loading.
  5. Missing the .controller.js suffix—V2 Smart Templates automatically append .controller.js during module resolution. Naming our file CustomTilesController.js will cause a silent load failure.
  6. Using addContent() on GenericTile — GenericTile does not have an addContent() method; the required aggregation is tileContent.  Always define it in the constructor. Attempting to use .addContent() will throw a TypeError. 

 

Conclusion

This approach allows us to deliver high-visibility analytical data to our end-users without breaking core framework files or generated code, keeping our application clean, robust, and upgrade-safe. As always, test the fallback paths against our own app's layout before shipping—the patterns here are proven, but every customer's List Report has its own quirks.

entry.title: The Confirm button in My Purchase Requisitions app (F1639A) unavailable for prdct type group service
entry.content:
Hello sap Community, 

The "Confirm" button in the "My Purchase Requisitions" app (F1639A) appears to be available only when the purchase requisition product type group is "Material". In this case, it redirects users to the "Confirm Goods Receipt" app.
For purchase requisitions with the product type group "Service", the button is not available, and users must instead open the "Manage Service Entry Sheet" app (F2027).

Could the "Confirm" link be enhanced to redirect users to the "Confirm Goods Receipt" app when the product type group is "Material", and to the "Manage Service Entry Sheet" app when the product type group is "Service"?

Thank you

entry.title: Need APP to lookup component quantity under exist product
entry.content:
Greeting, SAP Expert

Our customer are looking for an APP to lookup the required component quantity under multiple highest level products. We tried several different apps, such as Find BOM for Component, Material Where-Used List in BOMs, or Explode bills of material, etc. however, non of them fullfill the requirement.

For example: 

                                 Product A1                                                                Product A2

         1 Component B1             1 Component B2                 1 Component B3           3 Component B1

1Comp C1     1Comp C2          1Comp C3        1Comp C1      1Comp C3    1Comp C2        1Comp C1     1Comp C2

Input C1
system can output:
Product A1 requires totally 2 C1, 
Product A2 requires 3 C1

Therefore, I'd like to ask SAP experts, is there any app in SAP public cloud able to acomplish this task?

Thank you!

entry.title: Re: Pushing data from datasphere to SFTP through replicat...
entry.content:
Hi,

This is the expected behavior of Replication Flows and is currently not configurable.

  1. Can the Initial folder be avoided?
    No. The Initial folder is automatically created for initial loads and cannot be removed or bypassed.
  2. Can the output file have a static name?
    No. The file name is system-generated and there is currently no option to define a custom/static name.

For more details, please refer to the SAP Help documentation for Replication Flows with SFTP targets

If this functionality is important for your use case, we recommend submitting an enhancement request via https://influence.sap.com/go/datasphere

Thanks,

Farah

entry.title: Re: ewm stock counting for batch with 0 stock
entry.content:
Click on create icon in /SCWM/PI_CREATE.

Enter the Material code, batch, Stock type, Disposal, Owner, Bin Detail and save it.. Upon saving it would create a Physical Inventory document for which you can perform the count in the subsequent step.

entry.title: Re: Function YTD() not working in Datasphere
entry.content:
Hello,

 

The YTD() function only works when the date dimension is correctly configured and used in the restricted measure.

If the YTD value is not shown in the data preview, please verify:

  • The date dimension is assigned with the correct time semantics.
  • The model contains data for the selected time period.
  • The YTD() function is applied to the correct date dimension.

 

Thanks,

Farah

entry.title: Using Refresh Token to Consume SAC Multi-Actions Public API
entry.content:
Introduction

 

SAC Multi-Actions let you chain planning operations — such as data action,  data locking, and version management — into a single execution sequence. While these are typically triggered from the SAC UI or scheduler, many enterprise scenarios demand programmatic invocation from external systems: as part of an SAP Integration Suite flow, or from a custom microservice running on SAP BTP.
 
The SAC Multi-Actions Public API enables this, but with an important constraint: it does not support OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials. All calls must run under a business user context. There are two common patterns to achieve non-interactive authentication while preserving user identity: the OAuth 2.0 refresh token flow (covered in detail in this post), and the OAuth2 SAML Bearer Assertion flow . This blog walks through how to use refresh tokens to consume the SAC Multi-Actions Public API end-to-end.

Understanding the Authentication Challenge

Why Not Client Credentials?

The SAC Multi-Actions API requires a business user context because planning operations involve data security, audit trails, and user-specific permissions. A service principal (client credentials) cannot own planning data or appear in audit logs. Therefore, every API call must carry a user identity.

The Role of Refresh Token

In OAuth 2.0, the authorization server issues two types of tokens: access token and refresh token. While access token grant short-lived access to protected resources, refresh token are used to acquire a new access token when the current one expires — without requiring the user to re-authenticate interactively.
 
For automated integrations, this means:
  • You perform interactive authentication once to obtain the initial access token and refresh token
  • Your application stores the refresh token securely
  • When the access token expires, your application uses the refresh token to get a new access token automatically
  • The integration continues running without human intervention
     

OAuth2 Refresh Token Flow

1. Enable External API Access on Multi-Actions

In SAC, open your Multi-Actions designer, click the settings icon, and check Allow External API Access. Save the changes and note down the Starter API URL — this is your Multi-Actions execution endpoint.
 

2. Create an OAuth Client in SAC

Navigate to System > Administration > App Integration and create a new OAuth client with these settings:
  • Purpose: Interactive Usage
  • Authorization Grant: Authorization Code
  • Redirect URI: https://oauth.usebruno.com/callback which is required by Bruno
  • Token Lifetime: Access tokens are short-lived, typically valid for 1 hour
  • Refresh Token Lifetime: Refresh tokens are valid for up to 180 days, and are used to obtain new access tokens       

After saving, copy the Client ID and Client Secret (the secret is only shown once). Also note the Token URL and Authorization URL displayed on the same page.

3. Configure OAuth 2.0 in Bruno

Go to the "Auth" tab of your request collection in Bruno, and configure a new OAuth 2.0 token with the following parameters:
  • Grant Type: Authorization Code
  • Callback URL: https://oauth.usebruno.com/callback 
  • Auth URL: The Authorization URL copied from SAC
  • Access Token URL: The Token URL copied from SAC
  • Client ID: Your OAuth client ID
  • Client Secret: Your OAuth client secret        

4. Obtain the Initial Refresh Token

After configuration is done, click on "Get Access Token" at the bottom of  Bruno Auth tab.  After logging on SAC in the pop-up window, both an access_token and a refresh_token should be fetched. The step is to perform the standard Authorization Code flow, and this is a one-time interactive step. 

Important: Securely store the refresh_token — this is your long-lived credential. Never hardcode it in your application or commit it to version control.

 

5. Use Refresh Token to Get a New Access Token

When the access token expires (or proactively before expiry in production implementations), call the token endpoint with grant_type=refresh_token, along with your stored refresh token. The request uses Basic authentication with your OAuth Client ID and Client Secret.

6: Fetch CSRF Token

The access token can be used to access Multi-Actions public APIs. In addition, SAC protects write operations with CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) tokens to prevent unauthorized cross-origin requests. Before sending HTTP POST requests (used for triggering Multi-Actions), you must first fetch a valid CSRF token.
 
Send a GET request to {your-tenant-url}/api/v1/csrf, including the header x-csrf-token: fetch. The CSRF token is returned in the x-csrf-token response header.
 
The CSRF token is bound to the current user session and can be reused for the lifetime of the access token. GET requests do not require a CSRF token.
  • Authorization:  Bearer <valid access token>        

 
7: Trigger Multi-Actions Execution
Use the valid access token and CSRF token in the following POST request to execute a Multi-Actions.
  • Authorization:  Bearer <valid access token>
  • Content-Type:  application/json
  • x-csrf-token:  <valid  csrf token> 

8: Get Multi-Actions Execution Result

Use the query status API to get the status of Multi-Actions execution result.

  • Authorization:  Bearer <valid access token>        

Complete Node.js Example

Below is a complete Node.js + Express example that implements the full refresh token flow, automatic CSRF token fetching, and proactive token refresh. The example uses server-side sessions for demonstration purposes; for production use, replace the in-memory session with a persistent store and a dedicated secrets manager.

Environment Configuration

Create a .env file in your project root with the following variables:

SAC_CLIENT_ID=your-oauth-client-id
SAC_CLIENT_SECRET=your-oauth-client-secret
SAC_BASE_URL=https://your-tenant-url
SAC_TOKEN_URL=https://your-tenant-token-url
SAC_AUTHORIZE_URL=https://your-tenant-authorize-url
REDIRECT_URI=http://localhost:3000/callback
PORT=3000
SESSION_SECRET=your-secure-session-secret

Full Source Code

require('dotenv').config();
const express = require('express');
const session = require('express-session');
const axios = require('axios');
const crypto = require('crypto');
const path = require('path');

const app = express();
const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000;

// SAC OAuth 2.0 credentials
const SAC_CLIENT_ID     = process.env.SAC_CLIENT_ID;
const SAC_CLIENT_SECRET = process.env.SAC_CLIENT_SECRET;
const SAC_BASE_URL      = process.env.SAC_BASE_URL;
const SAC_TOKEN_URL     = process.env.SAC_TOKEN_URL;
const SAC_AUTHORIZE_URL = process.env.SAC_AUTHORIZE_URL;
const REDIRECT_URI      = process.env.REDIRECT_URI || `http://localhost:${PORT}/callback`;

// Refresh 60 seconds before expiry to avoid mid-request failures
const TOKEN_REFRESH_BUFFER = 60;

app.use(express.json());
app.use(express.static(path.join(__dirname, 'public')));
app.use(session({
  secret: process.env.SESSION_SECRET || 'change-me-in-production',
  resave: false,
  saveUninitialized: false,
  cookie: { secure: false, maxAge: 60 * 60 * 1000 }
}));

// ── Helper: Generate Basic Auth header ───────────────────────────────────────
function getBasicAuth() {
  return Buffer.from(`${SAC_CLIENT_ID}:${SAC_CLIENT_SECRET}`).toString('base64');
}

// ── Helper: Check if access token needs refresh ──────────────────────────────
function tokenNeedsRefresh(req) {
  if (!req.session.accessToken || !req.session.tokenExpiresAt) return true;
  return Date.now() >= req.session.tokenExpiresAt - TOKEN_REFRESH_BUFFER * 1000;
}

// ── Helper: Fetch CSRF token from SAC ────────────────────────────────────────
async function fetchCsrfToken(accessToken) {
  const response = await axios.get(`${SAC_BASE_URL}/api/v1/csrf`, {
    headers: {
      Authorization: `Bearer ${accessToken}`,
      'x-csrf-token': 'fetch'
    }
  });
  return response.headers['x-csrf-token'];
}

// ── Step 1: Redirect user to SAC authorization page ─────────────────────────
app.get('/auth/sac', (req, res) => {
  if (!SAC_CLIENT_ID || !SAC_BASE_URL) {
    return res.status(500).json({ error: 'SAC_CLIENT_ID or SAC_BASE_URL not configured' });
  }

  const state = crypto.randomBytes(16).toString('hex');
  req.session.oauthState = state;

  const params = new URLSearchParams({
    response_type: 'code',
    client_id: SAC_CLIENT_ID,
    redirect_uri: REDIRECT_URI,
    state
  });

  res.redirect(`${SAC_AUTHORIZE_URL}?${params}`);
});

// ── Step 2: SAC redirects back with authorization code ──────────────────────
app.get('/callback', async (req, res) => {
  const { code, state, error, error_description } = req.query;

  if (error) {
    return res.redirect(`/?error=${encodeURIComponent(error_description || error)}`);
  }

  if (!state || state !== req.session.oauthState) {
    return res.redirect('/?error=invalid_state');
  }
  delete req.session.oauthState;

  if (!code) {
    return res.redirect('/?error=no_code');
  }

  try {
    // Exchange authorization code for access + refresh tokens
    const tokenResponse = await axios.post(
      SAC_TOKEN_URL,
      new URLSearchParams({
        grant_type: 'authorization_code',
        code,
        redirect_uri: REDIRECT_URI
      }).toString(),
      {
        headers: {
          'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded',
          Authorization: `Basic ${getBasicAuth()}`
        }
      }
    );

    const { access_token, refresh_token, token_type, expires_in, scope } = tokenResponse.data;

    req.session.accessToken     = access_token;
    req.session.refreshToken    = refresh_token || null;
    req.session.tokenType       = token_type;
    req.session.tokenExpiresAt  = Date.now() + expires_in * 1000;
    req.session.scope           = scope;

    res.redirect('/?success=true');
  } catch (err) {
    const detail = err.response?.data?.error_description || err.response?.data?.error || err.message;
    console.error('Token exchange error:', detail);
    res.redirect(`/?error=${encodeURIComponent(detail)}`);
  }
});

// ── Helper: Refresh access token ─────────────────────────────────────────────
async function refreshAccessToken(req) {
  const rt = req.session.refreshToken;
  if (!rt) throw new Error('No refresh token available');

  try {
    const tokenResponse = await axios.post(
      SAC_TOKEN_URL,
      new URLSearchParams({
        grant_type:    'refresh_token',
        refresh_token: rt
      }).toString(),
      {
        headers: {
          'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded',
          Authorization:  `Basic ${getBasicAuth()}`
        }
      }
    );

    const { access_token, refresh_token, expires_in, scope } = tokenResponse.data;

    req.session.accessToken    = access_token;
    req.session.tokenExpiresAt = Date.now() + expires_in * 1000;
    req.session.scope          = scope;

    // Handle token rotation: update stored refresh token if a new one is issued
    if (refresh_token) {
      req.session.refreshToken = refresh_token;
    }

    return access_token;
  } catch (err) {
    const detail = err.response?.data?.error_description || err.response?.data?.error || err.message;
    console.error('Refresh token exchange error:', detail);
    throw new Error(`Token refresh failed: ${detail}`);
  }
}

// ── Middleware: Ensure valid access token before API calls ───────────────────
async function requireValidToken(req, res, next) {
  try {
    if (tokenNeedsRefresh(req)) {
      await refreshAccessToken(req);
    }
    next();
  } catch (err) {
    res.status(401).json({ error: err.message });
  }
}

// ── API: Get current token status ───────────────────────────────────────────
app.get('/api/token', (req, res) => {
  if (!req.session.accessToken) {
    return res.status(401).json({ authenticated: false });
  }
  const token = req.session.accessToken;
  res.json({
    authenticated: true,
    token_type:    req.session.tokenType,
    expires_in:    Math.max(0, Math.floor((req.session.tokenExpiresAt - Date.now()) / 1000)),
    scope:         req.session.scope,
    token_preview: `${token.slice(0, 6)}${'*'.repeat(6)}${token.slice(-6)}`,
    token_length:  token.length,
    has_refresh_token: !!req.session.refreshToken
  });
});

// ── API: Manually trigger token refresh ─────────────────────────────────────
app.post('/api/refresh', requireValidToken, async (req, res) => {
  try {
    await refreshAccessToken(req);
    const token = req.session.accessToken;
    const newRt = req.session.refreshToken;
    res.json({
      success: true,
      token_preview: `${token.slice(0, 6)}${'*'.repeat(6)}${token.slice(-6)}`,
      token_length:  token.length,
      expires_in:    Math.floor((req.session.tokenExpiresAt - Date.now()) / 1000),
      refresh_token_rotated: !!req.session.refreshToken,
      new_refresh_token_preview: `${newRt.slice(0, 6)}${'*'.repeat(6)}${newRt.slice(-6)}`
    });
  } catch (err) {
    res.status(500).json({ error: err.message });
  }
});

// ── API: Trigger multi-action execution ─────────────────────────────────────
app.post('/api/multiactions/:multiActionsId/executions', requireValidToken, async (req, res) => {
  try {
    // Automatically fetch CSRF token before POST request
    const csrfToken = await fetchCsrfToken(req.session.accessToken);

    const response = await axios.post(
      `${SAC_BASE_URL}/api/v1/multiActions/${encodeURIComponent(req.params.multiActionsId)}/executions/`,
      req.body,
      {
        headers: {
          Authorization: `Bearer ${req.session.accessToken}`,
          'Content-Type': 'application/json',
          'x-csrf-token': csrfToken,
          Accept: 'application/json'
        }
      }
    );
    res.status(response.status).json(response.data);
  } catch (err) {
    const status = err.response?.status || 500;
    const errorDetail = err.response?.data?.messages?.[0]?.message || err.response?.data || err.message;
    res.status(status).json({ error: errorDetail });
  }
});

// ── API: Get multi-action execution status ──────────────────────────────────
app.get('/api/multiactions/:multiActionsId/executions/:executionId', requireValidToken, async (req, res) => {
  try {
    const response = await axios.get(
      `${SAC_BASE_URL}/api/v1/multiActions/${encodeURIComponent(req.params.multiActionsId)}/executions/${encodeURIComponent(req.params.executionId)}`,
      {
        headers: {
          Authorization: `Bearer ${req.session.accessToken}`,
          Accept: 'application/json'
        }
      }
    );
    res.json(response.data);
  } catch (err) {
    const status = err.response?.status || 500;
    const errorDetail = err.response?.data?.messages?.[0]?.message || err.response?.data || err.message;
    res.status(status).json({ error: errorDetail });
  }
});

// ── API: Logout and clear session ───────────────────────────────────────────
app.post('/api/logout', (req, res) => {
  req.session.destroy(() => res.json({ success: true }));
});

app.listen(PORT, () => {
  console.log(`Server running at http://localhost:${PORT}`);
  const missing = ['SAC_CLIENT_ID', 'SAC_CLIENT_SECRET', 'SAC_BASE_URL', 'SAC_TOKEN_URL']
    .filter(k => !process.env[k]);
  if (missing.length) {
    console.warn(`WARNING: Missing environment variables: ${missing.join(', ')}. Copy .env.example to .env.`);
  }
});

Conclusion

Using refresh token to consume the SAP Analytics Cloud Multi-Actions Public API provides a robust, automated approach for integrating SAC planning operations into your enterprise workflows. By separating the initial user authentication from ongoing API access, refresh token enable long-running integrations without compromising security or requiring interactive logins.
 
The key to success lies in implementing proper token lifecycle management: secure storage of credentials, proactive refresh before access token expiry, correct handling of token rotation, and graceful degradation when the refresh token itself expires.
 
Whether you're triggering Multi-Actions orchestrating planning cycles from SAP Integration Suite, or building custom planning applications on BTP, the refresh token flow ensures your SAC integrations remain authenticated and operational around the clock.

entry.title: Purchasing info record not updated after creation of PO
entry.content:
I created purchase order environment using a purchasing info record and changed the price in the purchase order. I selected the Info Update indicator.

Afterwards, I executed the job Determine and Update the Price of Purchasing Info Record. The job does not show any results.

I can see that the purchase order number has been added as the reference document in the purchasing info record. However, when I create a new purchase order using the same purchasing info record, the old price is still proposed.

Could you please confirm whether this is the expected system behavior? If not, could you advise which prerequisites or configuration settings are required for the updated price to be used in subsequent purchase orders?

entry.title: Re: trying to build a career in sap supply chain manageme...
entry.content:
Hi @harini_karthikeyanf0bfd5 

Welcome to the SAP Learning Group, nice to meet you

If you are new to SAP and interested in building a career in Supply Chain Management (SCM), I would recommend starting with a structured learning path rather than jumping directly into certification preparation. 

A good approach might be:

  1. Visit our Supply Chain Management learn by product site  

    • There you find learning materials to learn how to build connected, risk-resilient, and sustainable supply chains, understand core business processes such as procurement, inventory management, production planning, warehousing, and logistics.
  2. Browse the supply chain management capabilities
    • Explore the learning paths, they provide a structured introduction and help you understand the various SCM solutions available in SAP.

  3. Gain hands-on experience

    • If possible, use SAP practice systems and exercises to become familiar with the applications and processes. Practical experience is extremely valuable when starting an SAP career.

  4. Connect with other learners

    • Consider joining the relevant SAP Learning Groups to exchange experiences, ask questions, and learn from community members who are already working in the SCM domain, get support you, answer questions, and share practical insights from their experiences. 
      For this, I recommend joining the Supply Chain Management Learning Group, where you can connect with fellow learners and professionals, exchange experiences, ask questions, and receive support from a community that shares your interest in SAP Supply Chain Management.

  5. Consider certification later

    • Once you have completed the foundational learning content and gained some practical understanding, you can evaluate whether an SAP certification aligns with your career goals. 
Of course, these are only recommendations. The best learning path depends on your individual career goals, your current role, the industry you work in, and the direction you would like to take in your SAP career.

Best of luck with your learning journey!
Kind regards
Margit

Thanks

Naveen

entry.title: Export SAP Integration Suite Inventory to Excel: Two Python Scripts for CPI iFlows and API Proxies.
entry.content:
If you have ever been asked how many iFlows sit on a tenant, or which API proxies belong to which product, you already know how it goes. You open the UI, click into one package at a time, and copy names into a spreadsheet by hand. It takes a while, and the list is out of date the moment someone adds a new flow. I got tired of doing this, so I wrote a couple of small scripts to do it for me.

There are two scripts. Each one reads a different part of SAP Integration Suite and writes the result straight into an Excel file. They use the standard public OData APIs with an OAuth client credential, so nothing is installed on the tenant. You run them from your own machine and you get a clean spreadsheet at the end.

Here is the one thing to keep straight while you read. There are two separate scripts for two separate jobs:

  • cpi_inventory_export.py reads Cloud Integration and lists every integration package and the iFlows inside each one.
  • apim_inventory_export.py reads API Management and lists every API proxy and the product it belongs to.

They look alike and run the same way, but they connect to different services in BTP and need different roles. To avoid any mix up, I have written each one as its own part below. The download link for both is at the end.

What each script produces

Script Reads from Output file Sheets you get

cpi_inventory_export.pyCloud IntegrationCPI_Inventory.xlsxPackages, iFlows
apim_inventory_export.pyAPI ManagementAPIM_Inventory.xlsxAPI Proxies, Products

The output is plain and easy to filter. The CPI file gives you one row per iFlow with its package name, so you can sort or pivot however you like. The APIM file gives you one row per proxy with the product it sits in, which is the view most people are really after.

Before you start

A few things need to be in place. These are the same for both scripts.

  • You can log in to the SAP BTP cockpit for your subaccount.
  • Cloud Integration or API Management is active in your Integration Suite tenant, depending on which script you want to run.
  • Python 3 is installed on your machine. You can check by opening a Command Prompt and running the command below.
python --version

If you see a version number come back, you are good. If the command is not recognised, install Python from python.org and tick the option to add it to PATH during setup.

Both scripts also need two small Python libraries. You only install these once, and the same two work for both scripts.

python -m pip install requests openpyxl

Part 1: Cloud Integration packages and iFlows (cpi_inventory_export.py)

This is the script that answers the question, what packages and iFlows do we have. It talks to the Process Integration Runtime service.

Step 1: Create the service instance

In your subaccount, open Service Marketplace and search for Process Integration Runtime. Open the tile and choose Create. On the Basic Info screen set these values:

  • Plan: api. This one matters. The integration-flow plan is only for sending messages to a deployed iFlow, and it will not list your packages. If you pick the wrong plan you get a 404 later.
  • Runtime Environment: Cloud Foundry
  • Space: your space, for example dev
  • Instance Name: cpi-extract

Step 2: Add the role

Move to the Parameters step. In the Roles field select WorkspacePackagesRead and keep the grant type as Client Credentials. This role is what lets the script read your packages and iFlows. If you leave it out, the API later tells you it cannot find the entity set, which is a confusing error for what is really just a missing role. Choose Create.

Step 3: Create the service key

Open the cpi-extract instance, go to Service Keys, and create one. Name it something like cpi-extract-key and leave the parameters box as the empty braces. Open the key in JSON view and copy these four values:

  • url
  • tokenurl
  • clientid
  • clientsecret

The Cloud Integration url usually has it-cpi in the host name and often ends with -rt. Make a mental note of that, because it is the easiest way to tell it apart from the API Management url in Part 2.

Step 4: Fill in the script and run it

Open cpi_inventory_export.py in any editor. Near the top, paste your four values inside the quotes and save. Keep the quotes in place.

CLIENT_ID = "your clientid"
CLIENT_SECRET = "your clientsecret"
TOKEN_URL = "your tokenurl"
API_URL = "your url"

Open a Command Prompt, move into the folder that holds the script, and run it.

cd "C:\path\to\your\folder"
python cpi_inventory_export.py

When it finishes you will see a line like Done. 12 packages exported to CPI_Inventory.xlsx. Open the file and you will find a Packages sheet and an iFlows sheet, sitting in the same folder as the script.

Part 2: API Management proxies and products (apim_inventory_export.py)

This is the script that answers the question, what API proxies do we have and which product is each one in. It talks to the API Management, API portal service, which is a different service from Part 1.

Step 1: Create the service instance

In your subaccount, open Service Marketplace and search for API Management, API portal. Take care to pick this tile and not the developer portal tile next to it. Open it and choose Create. On the Basic Info screen set these values:

  • Plan: apiportal-apiaccess
  • Runtime Environment: Cloud Foundry
  • Space: your space, for example dev
  • Instance Name: api-extract

 

Step 2: Add the role

Move to the Parameters step. Here the role is entered as JSON, not picked from a list. This is the step people most often miss, and skipping it is what leaves you with an empty service key later. Type the role, then choose Create.

{ "role": "APIPortal.Administrator" }

Do not have the Administrator role? You do not need it for this. The Administrator role allows create, update, and delete, but this script only reads. If you cannot or should not use Administrator, use the read-only role instead:

{ "role": "APIPortal.Guest" }

APIPortal.Guest gives read-only access to the API portal, which is all the script needs to list your proxies and products. It is also the safer choice if you are following least privilege, so if you are unsure, start with Guest. Whichever role you choose, it goes here on the Parameters step of the instance, not on the service key.

Step 3: Create the service key

Open the api-extract instance, go to Service Keys, and create one. Name it something like api-extract-key and leave the parameters box as the empty braces. Open the key in JSON view and copy these four values:

  • url
  • tokenUrl
  • clientId
  • clientSecret

The API Management url has the word apiportal in the host name. This is the biggest difference from Part 1. If you accidentally use the Cloud Integration host, the one with it-cpi, you will get a 404, so double check you copied the url from this key and not the CPI one.

Step 4: Fill in the script and run it

Open apim_inventory_export.py in any editor. Near the top, paste your four values inside the quotes and save. Keep the quotes in place.

CLIENT_ID = "your clientId"
CLIENT_SECRET = "your clientSecret"
TOKEN_URL = "your tokenUrl"
API_URL = "your url"

Open a Command Prompt, move into the folder that holds the script, and run it.

cd "C:\path\to\your\folder"
python apim_inventory_export.py

When it finishes you will see a line like Done. 1 proxies and 1 products exported to APIM_Inventory.xlsx. Open the file and you will find an API Proxies sheet and a Products sheet.

Quick comparison of the two scripts

If you take away one thing from this blog, take away this table. It shows exactly where the two scripts differ, so you always know which value belongs to which script.

  cpi_inventory_export.py apim_inventory_export.py

Service in BTPProcess Integration RuntimeAPI Management, API portal
Planapiapiportal-apiaccess
RoleWorkspacePackagesReadAPIPortal.Administrator, or APIPortal.Guest for read-only
url containsit-cpiapiportal
Output fileCPI_Inventory.xlsxAPIM_Inventory.xlsx

Common issues

These are the problems you are most likely to hit, with the quick fix for each.

  • Service key is empty. The role was not set on the instance. Recreate the instance with the role, then make a new key. For API Management, remember you can use APIPortal.Guest if Administrator is not available.
  • 404 not found. Wrong host or wrong plan. Use the url from the correct service key, and check the plan.
  • Entity set not found on CPI. Add WorkspacePackagesRead and create a new key.
  • 401 unauthorized. One of the four values is mistyped. Copy them again from the key.
  • No module named requests. Run the pip install command again.
  • SyntaxError when you run the command. You are inside the Python shell, shown by three greater than signs. Type exit() first, then run the command from the normal prompt.

Download the scripts

Both scripts are on GitHub with a short README. Download them here and follow along.

Download: cpi_inventory_export.py

Download: apim_inventory_export.py

If this saved you some clicking, drop a comment and tell me what you would like to see next. I am happy to extend the scripts, for example to add deployment status or the last changed date for each artifact..

entry.title: Re: Route Determination for One-Time Customers in S/4HANA...
entry.content: Hi Dominik, Thanks for your reply. Yes, we are receiving input file with one time BP address details but not Transportation zone. List of transportation zone is set up in supplier system so customer cannot send TZ in the inbound file. We are using extensibility to use postal code and add transportation zone in address detail and create sales order. Still, route is not determining in order but after creation, manually updating ship to is triggering auto route determination. I understood from SAP community portal that One time customer does not hold master data in the system so in initial EDI run, system won't go for auto route determination even TZ is updated in the address details. Please let me know if you are aware about any BADI to validate Order details before saving which can trigger route determination. Thanks for your help.

entry.title: SAP Note Creation Process for Customer Incident
entry.content:
Introduction :-
When customer raises a production-related incident in the ServiceNow tool, they provide issue details, scenario snapshots, and system information to help debug the reported issue. Based on this input after resolving the issue and testing in Internal systems. I followed a defined process to create the corresponding SAP Note and release note either tailored to the specific customer or applicable to all customers. 

 The process covers:

  • Creating the Request and associated Task 
  • Releasing the Transport Request (TR) 
  • Adding Corrective Instructions (Support Pack references) 
  • Creating and structuring the SAP Note 
  • Pilot Release and General Release process  

What is I7P?

I7P is SAP’s internal support and knowledge management system that serves as the central repository for SAP Notes. It is used by SAP teams to create, maintain, and track solutions for product issues before they are published to customer-facing support channels. As the backend source of SAP Notes, I7P helps ensure accurate technical knowledge, fixes, and support documentation are delivered to customers and also I7P system help to create Transport Request , Notes and to add support Pack based on customer system version. 

Transport Request and Task

Transport Request is the main container that holds all development changes. It is created whenever changes are made to repository objects such as programs, classes, and tables.

Task is a child object under a Transport Request. 

  • Must be released first before releasing the parent request.  
  • Tasks cannot be transported independently. 

Steps to Create ABAP Corrective Measures

Step 1: Open ABAP Corr. Measures

Log in to the I7P system and click ABAP Corr. Measures.

Step 2: Provide the Required Details

Fill in the required fields — Application Component, Short Text, Priority, and Software Component — and select the SAP Note radio button.

Step 3: Select Components and Systems

Select the components and systems where the changes are needed, then click Continue with System Selection.

Step 4: Choose the Transport Request Type

Select the type of Transport Request required based on the object changes — Workbench or Customizing.

The correction request is then created, as shown below:

Note: All Transport Requests and their associated tasks can be reviewed in transactions SE09 and SE10. Once all changes have been locked in the Transport Request, open transaction SE09, enter the user name, and click Display. Select the task and run a Consistency Check. If any errors are found, the task cannot be released until they are resolved. After a successful consistency check, click the Transport icon, as shown below.

Step 5: Retrofitting

Retrofit is a synchronization process used to copy changes made in a production or maintenance system into a parallel project or development system.

Go to transaction SCWB, enter the user ID (the request should be under the name of the person performing the retrofit), and click Execute.

Double-click the request whose changes need to be distributed to other systems, then select the request and click the Distribute icon, as shown below. All object changes are transported to the respective systems, and the system releases the tasks.

The system displays a success message confirming that the changes were distributed successfully.

Step 6 : Test Transport

Test Transport is the process of importing development changes from the DEV system into QAS for testing and validation prior to production deployment.

Right-click the request for the relevant system, select Process Item, then Generate Test Transport.

Press Enter, and a success message will appear.

 

Step 7 : Creating Correction Instructions and Adding the Support Package

A pop-up screen will appear — click Enter.

The correction request is created as shown below. Repeat this step for all the systems.

Double-click the created correction instruction to review the changes made to the object, add the dependent SAP Note, and add the Support Package.

Double-click the object name to view the code changes (inserted and deleted blocks).

Click Validity to add the Support Package (SP) — select n+1, where n represents 12 months

Select the block and press Enter.

Check and Release the Correction Instruction. 

Note: Correction instructions must be created for all the systems.

Step 8 : Creating the SAP Note

Double-click the created SAP Note and provide the symptom, solution, and objects changed for the issue. Add attachments if needed, and mention the relevant components in the SAP Note header.

Pilot Release – A customer number is added to the SAP Note so that the changes apply only to the designated customer and do not impact others.

General Release – The changes implemented through the SAP Note become applicable to all customers.

Step 9 : Final Step

After completing all development activities, SAP Note creation, correction instruction creation, and testing across various scenarios, the changes are thoroughly validated. Once all validations are successful, the SAP Note and correction request are assigned to the senior consultants for final review and release.

Conclusion 

By reading this blog,one will have a knowledge on Transport Request, Task, Retrofitting, Transporting changes from Development system to Quality system, Steps to create SAP Note, Release note.
Once the release note is issued, the customer can apply the note via SNOTE transaction and the reported issue will be resolved.

Thank you for reading!!
