entry.title: Job Classification (EC) vs. Job Role (JPB): Two Objects You Have to Keep in Sync Yourself
entry.content:
Introduction

A question that comes up in almost every design workshop where Employee Central (EC) and Job Profile Builder (JPB) are both in scope: we already have Job Classifications in EC, so why do we need Job Roles in JPB? They sound interchangeable, and teams often assume maintaining one takes care of the other. It doesn't. Here's what each object is for, how they connect, and why a simplified 1:1 job architecture still means double maintenance.

The two objects and what they do

Job Classification is a Foundation Object in Employee Central. It's the administrative side of the job - assigned to positions and to Job Information, carrying attributes like pay grade, employee class and standard hours that drive business rules, reporting and integrations.

Job Role belongs to Job Profile Builder (Families and Roles). It's the talent side. It holds the job profile content: description, responsibilities, competencies and skills. This is what Recruiting, Performance, Succession and Career Development actually consume.

How they connect

The two are linked through a mapping maintained in Manage Job Profile Content, or via the Role–Job Code import template for bulk changes. The chain is simple: an employee holds a Job Classification in Job Information, the classification is mapped to a Role, and through the Role the employee gets their job profile.

Why the simplified design doesn't remove the effort

Many of us choose a simplified architecture: one Job Classification = one Job Role, same name, same code. Clean and easy to explain. But there is no automatic sync between the two objects. Creating a Job Classification does not create a Job Role, and renaming one does not rename the other.

Every job catalog change is therefore a three-step exercise: update the Job Classification in EC, update the Job Role in JPB, and maintain the Role-to-Classification mapping. Miss a step and nothing errors out - things just quietly break. An employee with no job profile, competencies missing from a performance form, a requisition pulling stale content.

What has worked for me: one owner and one intake process for catalog changes covering both objects, aligned external codes from day one, imports for mass changes, and a periodic reconciliation of active classifications against mapped roles.

Conclusion

Job Classification and Job Role answer two different questions: "what is this person administratively assigned to?" vs. "what does this job require?" SuccessFactors keeps them as independent objects with only a manually maintained mapping between them. A 1:1 simplified architecture is still a sound choice; it just doesn't eliminate the dual maintenance, so plan your governance around it rather than discovering the drift later.

entry.title: Getting Started with the New Git Access Feature in SAP Cloud Integration
entry.content:
SAP Integration Suite recently added a built-in Git Access capability to Cloud Integration, letting you connect a GitHub repository directly to your tenant and push or pull integration artifacts natively from the Design workspace — no external CI/CD tooling or on-premise Git agent needed. In this post, I'll walk through the complete round-trip end to end: generating a GitHub token, connecting the repository, pushing an iFlow, merging it through a pull request, and importing it back into a new package via Git Import. Along the way I'll also flag a permissions error I ran into (GIT_OPERATION_FORBIDDEN) and how to avoid it, so you can get this working on the first try.

 

Part 1 — Preparation in GitHub

Step 1: Create a Fine-Grained Personal Access Token (PAT)

SAP CI authenticates to GitHub using a Personal Access Token, so the first stop is GitHub itself.

 

  1. Go to GitHub → Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens → Fine-grained tokens.
  2. Click Generate new token.
  3. Under Repository access, choose Only select repositories and pick the repository you want to connect (in my case, cicd-intsuite-builtin).

 

⚠️Tip / lesson learned: When I first generated a token without explicitly granting Contents (Read and write) permission on the repository, SAP CI was able to connect to the repo (fetching it for the picklist worked fine), but as soon as I tried to push an artifact I got:

Error in fetching branches from the given repository.

GIT_OPERATION_FORBIDDEN

Make sure you explicitly add repository permissions for at least:

  • Contents: Read and write
  • Metadata: Read-only (added automatically)
  • Pull requests: Read and write

 

Without the write permission on Contents, any push attempt from SAP CI will fail with the error above, even though the repository shows up correctly during setup.

  1. Generate the token and copy it immediately — GitHub only shows it once.

 

Step 2: Note the Repository Clone URL

You'll need the HTTPS clone URL for the repo, e.g.:

https://github.com/<your-username>/<your-repo>.git

That's everything needed on the GitHub side for now — the rest of the initial setup happens in SAP CI.

 

Part 2 — Connecting the Repository in SAP Cloud Integration

Step 3: Open Git Access Settings

In your Integration Suite tenant, navigate to:

Settings → Integrations → Git Access

You'll see a Repository Details table (empty the first time) with an Add button.

 

Step 4: Add the Repository

Click Add to open the Add Repository dialog and fill in:

 

  • URL: your repo's clone URL (e.g. https://github.com/<your-username>/cicd-intsuite-builtin.git)
  • Personal Access Token (PAT): the fine-grained token generated in Step 1
  • Name: a friendly name (e.g. cicd-intsuite-builtin)
  • Description: optional, but useful for documenting the repo's purpose

Click Connect to validate the credentials, then Add, and finally Save on the Git Access screen. You should see a confirmation toast: "Repositories saved." The repository now appears in the Repository Details table with its URL, description, and the user who added it.

 

Part 3 — Pushing an iFlow from SAP CI to GitHub

Step 5: Push an Artifact

Open an integration package containing an iFlow you'd like to version-control (e.g. Test Package for GIT Access), go to the Artifacts tab, and use the artifact's Push action.

In the Push dialog you'll select:

  • The Personal Access Token (pre-filled once configured)
  • The Repository
  • The Repository Branch — you can push to an existing branch or type a new branch name (e.g. dev) to create one on the fly
  • A Commit Message

If your PAT permissions are correctly scoped (see the warning in Step 1), the push succeeds and the artifact status updates to show something like:

Created | Branch: dev | Repository: cicd-intsuite-builtin

 

Step 6: Verify on GitHub

Switch over to GitHub and confirm the new branch was created with your commit — you'll typically see a banner like "dev had recent pushes X seconds ago" along with a Compare & pull request button.

 

Part 4 — Merging the Change in GitHub

Step 7: Open a Pull Request

Click Compare & pull request. Confirm the base branch (main) and compare branch (dev), add a title and description, and click Create pull request.

 

 

Step 8: Merge the Pull Request

Since there are no conflicts, GitHub will show "No conflicts with base branch — Merging can be performed automatically." Click Merge pull request, confirm the merge commit message, and click Confirm merge. Optionally delete the now-merged dev branch.

 

 

At this point, your iFlow — pushed from SAP CI — is committed on main in GitHub, complete with the full folder structure (META-INF, src/main/resources/scenarioflows/integrationflow, .project, metainfo.prop, and the .iflw file itself).

 

Part 5 — Importing an iFlow Back into SAP CI via Git Import

This is where the round-trip closes: you can pull artifacts from GitHub into a (new or existing) package.

Step 9: Add Integration Flow via Git Import

Create or open a package (e.g. Test Package for Import using GIT Access), go to Artifacts → Add → Integration Flow. In the dialog, three source options appear: Create, Upload, and Git Import. Select Git Import and provide:

 

  • Personal Access Token
  • Repository Name (chosen from the ones you've connected)
  • Repository URL (auto-filled)
  • Branch (e.g. main)
  • Integration Flow — a dropdown populated with the iFlows found in that branch/repo (in this example, Test_IFlow_for_GIT_Access)

 

Step 10: Add and Verify

Click Add (or Add and Open in Editor to jump straight into design mode). The iFlow is imported into your package, fully editable, and ready to be worked on — completing a clean push → PR → merge → import cycle entirely through SAP CI's native Git Access, with GitHub as the source of truth.

 

Summary

Where.     What happens

GitHub

Generate fine-grained PAT with Contents: Read & write, note repo URL

SAP CI

Settings → Integrations → Git Access → Add repository (URL + PAT)

SAP CI

Push iFlow from package → creates/updates a branch in GitHub

GitHub

Review, open PR, merge into main

SAP CI

Add Integration Flow → Git Import → pick repo/branch/iFlow → Add

 

Conclusion

In this post, we covered how to set up and use SAP Cloud Integration's native Git Access feature end to end — from generating a properly-scoped fine-grained GitHub PAT, connecting a repository under Settings, pushing an iFlow to a branch, and merging it through a standard GitHub pull request, to pulling it back into a brand-new package with Git Import. Along the way, you also learned how to avoid the GIT_OPERATION_FORBIDDEN error by making sure your PAT has Contents and Pull request permissions — a small but easy-to-miss detail that can otherwise cost you a fair bit of troubleshooting time. With this native capability, teams can now manage version control for their integration artifacts using standard Git workflows, without standing up separate CI/CD infrastructure.

I hope this walkthrough saves you some trial and error. If you have questions, run into a different error message, or have tips of your own to add, please drop them in the comments below — I'd love to hear about your experience with this feature.

Have you tried this feature yet? Let me know in the comments if you've run into other edge cases worth documenting.

entry.title: Launching the APAC Edition of Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise - A 7 part Webinar Series
entry.content:
We are excited to announce the Asia Edition of the Architecting the Autonomous Enterprise webinar series.

A structured, seven-session programme designed specifically for architects who need to understand, evaluate, and act on SAP's Autonomous Enterprise strategy.  SAP's unique position (50 years of industry knowledge, the Knowledge Graph, semantically rich business data, and built-in governance) provides the structural foundation that generic AI approaches cannot replicate. But understanding how to architect for this shift requires more than reading documentation.

This series gives you direct access to the people building the platform, the reference architectures guiding decisions, and the practical patterns you need to translate strategy into delivery.

The Full Programme: Seven Sessions, One Architecture Journey

Register now: Register for the Series

 

No. Date Title Speakers

124 August 2026Unpacking the Autonomous Enterprise: The What, Why, and How for ArchitectsSindhu Gangadharan
21 September 2026Architecting From Intent to Action: Joule Work and the Knowledge GraphRahul Lodhe, Sai Jaganath Remisetti
38 September 2026The Architect's Playbook for Building Your Own SAP AgentsJason Luo, Venkateshwaran Viswanathan, Gaurish Dessai
422 September 2026An Architect's Guide to AI Agent Lifecycle Management and the SAP Family of ModelsLaurenz Vogt, Mock Seng Chong
529 September 2026The AI North Star Architecture and AI Golden Path: From Reference to RealityPVN Pavankumar, Paul Craigie
66 October 2026Data Foundations for an Autonomous Enterprise: From Fragmented Systems to Trusted ContextNiclas Schautkotter, Alan Beven
713 October 2026The Role of Enterprise Architects in Driving the Autonomous EnterprisePaul Kurchina, Dirk Treusch, Johannes Euler, Alex Aitken

Session 1 | 24 August 2026

Unpacking the Autonomous Enterprise: The What, Why, and How for Architects
Speaker: Sindhu Gangadharan
The keynote session that sets the stage. Sindhu Gangadharan unpacks the vision, architecture, and practical implications of the Autonomous Enterprise, covering three context gaps (process, data, governance) that keep enterprise AI stuck and how SAP addresses each structurally. You will be introduced to the five building blocks: Joule and Joule Work, the SAP Autonomous Suite, Industry AI, the SAP Business AI Platform, and agent-led transformation.
Key Takeaways:
  • Understand the three context gaps and how SAP addresses each structurally
  • Map the five building blocks to your current landscape
  • Recognise what changes in your architecture practice: new UX patterns, domain maps, cross-domain orchestration, and governance as a first-class principle
Register for the Series

 

 Session 2 | 1 September 2026

 

Architecting From Intent to Action: Joule Work and the Knowledge Graph
Speakers: Rahul Lodhe, Sai Jaganath Remisetti
The way people interact with enterprise systems is fundamentally changing. Instead of navigating menus, filling forms, and manually connecting processes, people will express intent, and the system will orchestrate the response. This session covers how Joule Work introduces a dynamic engagement layer that interprets intent, learns from the Knowledge Graph, surfaces contextual insights, automates routine work, and orchestrates AI agents. The Knowledge Graph provides the semantic backbone: entity understanding, master data relationships, and business context that ground every agent interaction in meaning rather than guesswork.
Key Takeaways:
  • Understand the architecture of Joule Work as the unified engagement layer
  • Learn how the Knowledge Graph provides semantic and knowledge context: the difference between data and meaning for AI reasoning
  • Identify what your systems need to expose (APIs, events, semantic metadata) for Joule Work to operate effectively
Register for Session 2

 Session 3 | 8 September 2026

The Architect's Playbook for Building Your Own SAP Agents
Speakers: Jason Luo, Venkateshwaran Viswanathan, Gaurish Dessai
The SAP Business AI Platform provides a unified environment for building AI agents grounded in business context, but where do you start? What decisions define whether your agent works in a demo or in production? This session is a hands-on walkthrough of building an agent from scratch using the SAP stack: from Joule Studio and the SAP Integration Suite to the AI Core runtime. You will see the full development lifecycle: defining agent capabilities, grounding them in business data and process context, testing behaviour, and preparing for governed deployment.
Key Takeaways:
  • Walk through the end-to-end agent development lifecycle on the SAP Business AI Platform
  • Understand decision points: when to use deterministic applications vs. agents vs. agentic workflows vs. Knowledge Graph queries
Register for the Series

 Session 4 | 22 September 2026

An Architect's Guide to AI Agent Lifecycle Management and the SAP Family of Models
Speakers: Laurenz Vogt, Mock Seng Chong
As AI agents proliferate across enterprise landscapes, organisations face a defining architectural challenge: how do you discover, govern, secure, and measure the business impact of hundreds of autonomous agents? This session explores SAP's end-to-end approach to AI agent lifecycle management through the SAP AI Agent Hub (built on LeanIX), covering discovery and registration through governance, observability, and decommissioning. It also takes a deeper look at the SAP Family of Models, including RPT 1.5 which delivers 3.5x higher prediction quality than generic LLMs because it is pre-trained with business context.
Key Takeaways:
  • Understand the full agent lifecycle (discover, register, govern, observe, decommission) and how it maps to existing EA governance frameworks
  • Learn how the SAP AI Agent Hub on LeanIX connects agents to business capabilities, value streams, and organisational context
  • Learn what makes SAP's purpose-built models structurally different from generic LLMs
  • Identify the governance operating model needed before scaling from tens to hundreds of agents
Register for the Series

Session 5 | 29 September 2026

 

The AI North Star Architecture and AI Golden Path: From Reference to Reality
Speakers: PVN Pavankumar, Paul Craigie
Every architect navigating the Autonomous Enterprise asks the same question: what does the target architecture actually look like, and how do I get there from where I am today? The AI-Native North Star Architecture defines how SAP envisions the convergence of AI, data, and applications across the full stack. The AI Golden Path complements this with a guided journey from idea to production-ready application, including detailed decision trees for agents vs. deterministic applications vs. Knowledge Graph queries vs. HANA Vector Engine vs. agentic workflows vs. code-based agents. This session brings both to life with live demonstrations and worked examples.
Key Takeaways:
  • Understand the AI-Native North Star Architecture as the target state
  • Use the AI Golden Path decision framework for consistent build-vs-buy and pattern-selection decisions
  • See live demonstrations of how reference patterns translate into working implementations
  • Learn how to use published assets from the SAP Architecture Center as alignment tools with stakeholders
Register for the Series

 Session 6 | 6 October 2026

Data Foundations for an Autonomous Enterprise: From Fragmented Systems to Trusted Context
Speakers: Niclas Schautkotter, Alan Beven
The difference between isolated AI pilots and sustainable AI at scale lies in the foundational data architecture. Without trusted, connected, contextual data, agents hallucinate, predictions fail, and governance becomes impossible. This session covers how SAP brings data and AI together with a deep dive into SAP Business Data Cloud, including zero-copy data extraction, delta share protocol, BW modernisation, and the evolving role of the Data Architect in an autonomous landscape.
Key Takeaways:
  • Understand why Data Context is the foundational layer of the Context System
  • Learn the architecture of SAP Business Data Cloud and BDC Connect, including zero-copy patterns
  • Understand the evolving role of the Data Architect: from ETL designer to context curator for AI agents
Register for the Series

Session 7 | 13 October 2026

 

The Role of Enterprise Architects in Driving the Autonomous Enterprise
Speakers: Paul Kurchina, Dirk Treusch, Johannes Euler, Alex Aitken
The closing session that brings it all together. When AI agents execute processes, orchestrate across domains, and make decisions grounded in business context, the architect's role shifts from system design to shaping the operating model, governance boundaries, and transformation roadmap. This session explores what the Autonomous Enterprise means for Enterprise Architects: how decision-making changes, what new competencies become essential, and how EAs position themselves as strategic connectors between business ambition and AI-enabled execution. Includes a complete series wrap-up and clear next steps.
Key Takeaways:
  • Understand how the EA role evolves: from system integrator to transformation orchestrator and governance steward
  • Learn new competency areas: agent lifecycle governance, context architecture design, cross-domain orchestration, AI-native operating models
  • Take away a consolidated view of the series: key patterns, architectural decisions, and reusable assets
  • Connect with a community of architects navigating the same transition
Register for the Series

 Who Should Attend

This series is designed for:
  • Enterprise Architects evaluating AI-native operating models and governance frameworks
  • Solution Architects designing agent-based solutions on the SAP stack
  • Data Architects building the contextual data foundations that AI agents depend on
  • Technology Leaders responsible for AI strategy and platform decisions across Asia-Pacific
Whether you are early in your AI journey or already building agents in production, each session provides actionable architectural guidance grounded in real platform capabilities.
 
All sessions run at 11:30 AM IST | 14:00 SGT | 16:00 AEST | 08:00 CEST, designed for Asia-Pacific time zones with accessibility for colleagues in Europe.
Registration is open now and is completely free for everyone!
 
Register for all sessions: url.sap/aewebinar-apac
 
Each session is independent, but the series is designed as a progressive architecture journey. Attending all seven sessions will give you the complete picture. Select the sessions relevant to your role, or join the full series to build a comprehensive understanding of what the Autonomous Enterprise means for your architecture practice.

entry.title: Creating a Custom Action to Export Selected Records to Excel Using Fiori Elements and CAP
entry.content:
Introduction

While working on a Fiori Elements application, I came across a requirement that initially seemed straightforward but wasn't available as a standard feature. The application already had the built-in Export to Excel option, but it always exported every row displayed in the table. The business users wanted something slightly different—they wanted to select only a few records and export just those.

My first thought was to implement a backend action that generated an Excel file and returned it to the UI. After exploring the available options, I realized that wasn't necessary. SAPUI5 already provides a Spreadsheet API that can generate Excel files directly in the browser, making the implementation much simpler while avoiding additional backend development.

In this blog, I'll walk through the approach I used to add a custom Export Selected button to a Fiori Elements List Report. By the end, you'll have a working example that exports only the selected rows and integrates naturally with the standard Fiori toolbar.

Step 1: Initialize the CAP Project

First, create a new directory for your project and initialize a standard SAP CAP application. Open your terminal and run:

mkdir employee
cd employee
cds init

Step 2: Define the Database Schema

Next, we will define the data model for our employees.

Create a file named schema.cds inside the db folder and add the following code:

// db/schema.cds
namespace company.hr;
 
entity Employees {
    key ID     : UUID;
    name       : String;
    department : String;
    role       : String;
    email      : String;
}

Step 3: Create the Service and UI Annotations

Now, expose the database entity as an OData service and add the basic Fiori UI annotations to define what columns will appear in our Fiori List Report table.

Create a file named cat-services.cds (typically in the srv folder) and add this code:

// srv/cat-services.cds
using { company.hr as db } from '../db/schema';
 
service EmployeeService {
    entity Employees as projection on db.Employees;
}
 
annotate EmployeeService.Employees with @(
    UI.LineItem : [
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Employee Name', Value : name },
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Department', Value : department },
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Role', Value : role },
        { $Type : 'UI.DataField', Label : 'Email', Value : email }
    ]
);

Step 4: Add Mock Data

To test our application, we need some dummy data. SAP CAP automatically loads CSV files that match the namespace and entity.

Create a folder named data inside your db folder, then create a file named company.hr-Employees.csv and paste the following:

ID;name;department;role;email
1;Alice Walker;Engineering;Backend Developer;alice@company.com
2;Bob Smith;Sales;Account Executive;bob@company.com
3;Charlie Davis;Engineering;Frontend Developer;charlie@company.com
4;Diana Prince;HR;HR Manager;diana@company.com

Step 5: Configure the Custom Action in the Page Map

Once your Fiori Elements application has been generated, the next step is to add a custom action to the table toolbar.

Follow these steps:

  1. Right-click on the webapp folder in your project.
  2. Select Show Page Map.
  3. In the Page Map, select the List Report page.
  4. Click the Edit (pencil) icon to modify the page.
  5. Under the Table / Table Toolbar section, click the + button next to Actions.
  6. Select Add Custom Action.

Configure the custom action with the following properties:

  • Label: Export Selected
  • Requires Selection: Enabled
  • Press Event: exportSelected

Step 6: Implement the Custom UI5 Export Logic

Now, we will write the logic that extracts the selected rows and converts them to an Excel file using the Fiori V4 API.

Open the newly generated controller extension file (ListReportExt.js) and replace its contents with the following:

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/export/Spreadsheet",
    "sap/m/MessageToast"
], function (Spreadsheet, MessageToast) {
    "use strict";
 
    return {
        exportSelected: function (oEvent) {
            
            // 1. Access the Fiori V4 API
            var oExtensionAPI = this.editFlow ? this : this.getExtensionAPI();
            
            // 2. Get only the rows selected by the user
            var aSelection = oExtensionAPI.getSelectedContexts();
 
            if (!aSelection || aSelection.length === 0) {
                MessageToast.show("Please select at least one employee to export.");
                return;
            }
 
            // 3. Extract the raw JSON data from those selected rows
            var aData = aSelection.map(function (oContext) {
                return oContext.getObject();
            });
 
            // 4. Map the Excel columns to your CDS database fields
            var aColumns = [
                { label: 'Employee Name', property: 'name' },
                { label: 'Department', property: 'department' },
                { label: 'Role', property: 'role' },
                { label: 'Email', property: 'email' }
            ];
 
            // 5. Configure the Excel Workbook properties
            var oSettings = {
                workbook: { 
                    columns: aColumns,
                    context: {
                        sheetName: 'Selected Employees'
                    }
                },
                dataSource: aData,
                fileName: "Custom_Employee_Export.xlsx",
                worker: false 
            };
 
            // 6. Build and Download the Excel file
            var oSheet = new Spreadsheet(oSettings);
            
            oSheet.build().then(function() {
                MessageToast.show("Excel export successful!");
            }).catch(function(sMessage) {
                MessageToast.show("Export failed: " + sMessage);
            }).finally(function () {
                oSheet.destroy(); // Destroy object to prevent memory leaks in the browser
            });
        }
    };
});

Step 7: Run and Test the Application

With the code in place, start your server by running:

cds watch

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

After running the application, select one or more employee records from the table. You'll notice that the Export Selected button is enabled only when records are selected. Clicking the button downloads an Excel file containing only the selected employees instead of the entire table.

 

 

 

 

Conclusion

What I liked most about this approach is that it keeps the implementation lightweight. Since the Excel file is generated in the browser using the SAPUI5 Spreadsheet library, there was no need to create a custom CAP action or write backend logic just for exporting data.

One thing I found useful was enabling Requires Selection for the custom action. Without it, users could click the button even when no rows were selected, resulting in unnecessary validation. Letting Fiori handle the button state provides a much better user experience.

Although this example exports employee data, the same approach can be used in almost any Fiori Elements application, such as Sales Orders, Purchase Orders, Expense Reports, or Products. If you've ever wanted to provide users with more control over what they export, this is a simple customization that's worth adding to your applications.

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite: як з'єднати SAP із будь-якою системою
entry.content:
📅 21 липня 2026  |  🏷 Інтеграція / SAP BTP

Сучасний IT-ландшафт компанії - це десятки систем: SAP, CRM, e-commerce, банківські API, логістичні платформи, хмарні сервіси. Змусити їх «говорити» між собою — завдання, яке вирішує SAP Integration Suite.

Що таке SAP Integration Suite?

SAP Integration Suite - це integration-as-a-service платформа в рамках SAP BTP, яка надає інструменти для побудови, моніторингу та управління інтеграціями між будь-якими системами: SAP-до-SAP, SAP-до-non-SAP, хмара-до-on-premise, API-менеджмент.

Ключові компоненти

•       Cloud Integration (iFlow) - головний інструмент: дизайн інтеграційних потоків у графічному редакторі

•       API Management - публікація, захист та моніторинг API

•       Open Connectors - готові конектори до 170+ зовнішніх сервісів (Salesforce, ServiceNow, Workday та ін.)

•       Integration Advisor - AI-асистент для маппінгу даних між різними форматами

•       Event Mesh - обмін подіями в реальному часі між системами

Як це працює на практиці

Уявіть: клієнт оформлює замовлення у вашому інтернет-магазині (Shopify). Дані автоматично потрапляють у SAP SD (замовлення на продаж), система перевіряє наявність товару у EWM, резервує запас, надсилає підтвердження клієнту через сервіс email-розсилки (SendGrid) і одночасно створює завдання у службі доставки (Nova Poshta API). Все це — один інтеграційний потік у SAP Integration Suite.

💡 Важлива деталь: SAP Integration Suite замінює старіший продукт SAP Process Integration (PI/PO). Якщо ваша компанія досі використовує PI/PO — плануйте міграцію. SAP підтримує PI/PO, але нових функцій там більше не з'являється.

Преднастроєні інтеграції: прискорюємо старт

SAP пропонує тисячі готових інтеграційних пакетів (Integration Packages) для найпоширеніших сценаріїв: S/4HANA ↔ SuccessFactors, S/4HANA ↔ Ariba, S/4HANA ↔ Concur, SAP ↔ Microsoft 365 тощо. Замість розробки з нуля — налаштування готового шаблону, що скорочує час і знижує ризики.

Кому варто розглянути SAP Integration Suite?

•       Компаніям із SAP, які хочуть інтегруватися з не-SAP системами

•       Тим, хто мігрує з PI/PO на сучасну хмарну платформу

•       Командам, що будують мікросервісну архітектуру навколо SAP

•       Бізнесам, що планують API-монетизацію своїх сервісів

Інтеграція - це не «разовий проект», а постійна дисципліна. SAP Integration Suite дає командам інструмент, де всі інтеграції видимі, моніторяться та управляються з одного місця. Це кардинально змінює ситуацію порівняно з «павутиною» point-to-point з'єднань, де ніхто не знає, що з чим пов'язано.

entry.title: IFRS - Migration of Contracts, Link to asset master, valuation continuation in S4 Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hi SAP experts!

I need to migrate lease contracts and associated assets for my client. From the existing documentation, I understand that these must be migrated individually and then linked again in Valuation Management. We have successfully completed the migration; the asset master records and asset values match in both systems.

However, the situation is a bit more complicated when it comes to the contracts.

Our goal is to link the contracts to the corresponding migrated assets and to continue the valuation with the correct values starting from the migration date. I suspect that we need to define the correct combination of the following parameters:

  1. Start of Consideration

- What date should be entered here? Start of contract?

 

  1. First Posting From

- Is this the first day after the migration date? The date from which postings begin in the new system?

- This field also exists in the contract master record. Do the two always have to match?

 

  1. Probable End

- Mandatory or optional?

 

  1. Valuation Behavior

- The options here are Relative Difference (2), New Start (3), Absolute Difference (4), and Reduction (5).

Only with New Start (3) I have the option to select an asset. However, if I want to reuse an existing migrated asset, I must select one of the other behaviors. These, in turn, do not allow me to select an asset. What should be done here?

 

  1. Asset

- As mentioned in point 4, I can only select the corresponding migrated asset when the valuation behavior is “New Start” (3). However, the help section states that a new asset will be used in that case. This appears to be a contradiction.

 

We’ve already tried and simulated a number of things ourselves, but so far none of the approaches has resulted in a clean continuation of the valuation. Is there a documented, standardized procedure anywhere for our requirement to continue the contracts with migrated assets after migration?

I’d appreciate any advice!

Best regards,

Rado

entry.title: 🇩🇪 Build Code-Based AI Agents on SAP Business Technology Platform (Berlin, Germany)
entry.content:
In this CodeJam, you will learn how to build state-of-the-art AI agents using the Generative AI Hub and Python. You will also gain the skills to create custom tools for your agents, including leveraging SAP's RPT-1 model. Finally, you will deploy your agents to SAP BTP (cloud foundry) and add them to Joule.In this CodeJam you can choose to build your agents with Python and CrewAI or LangGraph.

The CodeJam will include the following SAP technologies:
Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core

  • SAP RPT-1
  • Grounding Service
  • SAP AI Launchpad
  • Business Application Studio (Python)

Exercise Content:  https://github.com/SAP-samples/codejam-code-based-agents

Prerequisites: 

  • Bring your own laptop with power adapter and have a supported Chromium-based browser
  • It can be helpful to bring a tablet or second laptop, if you have one, to have the instructions on a separate screen.

Location and Date:

This is a free in-person event and is planned for Tuesday, September 15th, in Berlin from 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM local time. The event's language and content will be in English. 

Event Address: Zalando SE - BHW, Hedwig-Wachenheim-Straße 7, 10243 Berlin, Germany

  • A huge thank you to Zalando for hosting this event.
  • The instructor is SAP Developer Advocate @noravonthenen 

Please note that spaces are limited! We can only accommodate 35 attendees. If you want to attend, register by clicking the Yes button in the Will you be attending? section above to the right. Approximately 1 week prior to the event, you will receive an email to confirm your attendance, along with further instructions and details.

entry.title: File-based S/4HANA migration tool: Interoperability Review or Integration Certification?
entry.content:
I'm building an ISV solution and want to understand which certification track applies before I lock the architecture.

What it does: migrates financial data (balance sheet, P&L, WIP) from SAP R/3 to S/4HANA — reads a customer's own extract (e.g. CJI3), applies a customer-specific mapping, and generates journal-entry load files plus a validation report evidencing a zero delta.

  • Runs as a stateless multi-tenant SaaS on AWS, not on SAP BTP.
  • Read-only towards the source — no ABAP transport, no code in the customer landscape.
  • Today it produces files; it does not call SAP APIs directly.

It has already been used on a real enterprise migration; I'm now bringing it to market as a product.

Questions:

  1. Under the Jan-2026 framework, would a file-based tool like this fall under Interoperability Review rather than Integration Certification?
  2. If we switched the output to the released Journal Entry – Bulk Create API, would that on its own qualify us for Integration Certification — or does that track also require running on SAP BTP?

Pointers to documentation I may have missed are just as welcome as an answer.

entry.title: Why Every SAP Analytics Consultant Needs to Learn BDC in 2026 - My Personal Take
entry.content:
Over the past several years, my career has been quietly circled the same problem from different angles. I have configured sales compensation logic across multiple lines of business, implemented SuccessFactors modules, experimented with SAP BTP, written a master's thesis on low-code mobile development, earned an SAC Story Design certification and now spend my weeks living inside SAP Analytics Cloud and Power BI dashboards, with Qlik Sense in the mix too. Different projects, different tools, but really one long conversation between "business needs an answer" and "the data isn't cooperating." 

So when SAP Business Data Cloud started dominating every SAP Community feed and Sapphire keynote this year, I didn't read it as a another product launch to file away. I read it as SAP describing, almost exactly, the job I already do.

SAC Is Becoming Part of BDC And That's Not a Footnote

Here's the part that made me sit up: SAP Analytics Cloud is being folded into Business Data Cloud. SAC keeps its own storage for now, but it's gradually shifting toward BDC's storage layer, particularly Datasphere. That's not a minor backend detail for someone who builds stories and models in SAC but it's a signal that the platform I know is about to sit inside a bigger, more governed architecture and the way I design reports, connect data sources and think about "where does this number actually come from" is going to change with it.

If you're an analytics consultant and your product of choice is SAC, Power BI on top of SAP data or Qlik connected to SAP systems, you're not adjacent to this shift. You're standing right in the middle of it.

The Problem BDC Is Actually Solving Is One I have Lived

Every analytics engagement I have worked on whether it was SuccessFactors reporting, Power BI dashboards for finance or SAC stories has run into some version of the same wall: the data exists but it's scattered across systems, half-governed and everyone has a slightly different version of "the truth." You spend more time reconciling where a number came from than actually analyzing what it means.

BDC's whole premise is that this shouldn't be a per-project problem anymore. Instead of every consultant rebuilding the same data plumbing between SAP systems, Databricks, Snowflake or Big Query, SAP wants that context managed once, centrally, and trusted everywhere. When I first read that framing, it genuinely resonated and it's the exact gap I have spent years working around manually.

Why I'm Not Waiting for "Later" to Learn This

I have written SAP Community blogs before on SAP Build, on connecting BTP trial accounts to Eclipse, and more recently on hands-on impressions of the SAP Analytics Cloud Q1 2026 release. Each time, writing about a tool while I was still learning it forced me to actually understand it, not just use it on autopilot. BDC feels like the next one of those moments, except the stakes are bigger: this isn't a feature update, it's SAP redrawing the map that SAC, Datasphere and BW all sit on.

For consultants, early fluency in a shift like this tends to matter more than fluency in a shift that's already finished. Right now, most people I talk to know BDC exists but haven't gone past the keynote slide. That gap won't last SAP has already flagged that 2026 is the year BDC moves from architecture diagrams into real client pilots. I would rather be the person in the room who's actually poked at it than the person still asking what it is.

What This Means If You're Where I Am

If your background looks anything like mine SAC, Power BI, Qlik, SuccessFactors or general SAP consulting then here's my honest take on where to start:

  • Revisit your SAC projects with a BDC lens: Ask where your data actually lives today and what changes if that storage layer shifts toward Datasphere.
  • Get comfortable with the word "governance" again: BDC is as much a governance story as a technology one and that's traditionally been the less glamorous part of analytics work and which is exactly why it's undervalued and worth learning well.
  • Don't wait for a formal project to start learning: I didn't wait for a client to ask about SAP Build before writing about it. Poke around, read the architecture docs, write down your confusion because that's usually the fastest way through it.

I don't have a finished BDC implementation story to tell you yet but nobody outside a handful of pilot customers really does. What I have is a career built on exactly the problem BDC is trying to solve and enough scar tissue from reconciling data across SAP systems to know this is worth paying attention to early, not after it's already the standard.

If you're an analytics consultant reading this and thinking "I'll get to BDC eventually"  then I'd gently push back. The people who get to it first are the ones who get to shape how their clients adopt it, instead of just reacting to it.

 

I would love to hear from others in the SAP Community , if you've started exploring BDC in your SAC or Datasphere work, what's actually surprised you so far?

Drop it in the comments, I'm collecting real experiences, not just keynote takeaways.😊

entry.title: Open-Source ABAP Utility to Search Text Symbols (Text Elements) Using READ TEXTPOOL
entry.content:
Introduction

During a maintenance activity, I needed to locate a company name across several custom ABAP programs. Running the standard report **RPR_ABAP_SOURCE_SCAN** successfully found occurrences in the ABAP source code, but it did not return matches maintained as **Program Text Elements (Text Symbols)**.

The reason is that program Text Elements are stored in the program's text pool and are accessed using the ABAP statement **READ TEXTPOOL**, rather than being part of the ABAP source itself.

To solve this, I developed a small open-source utility that searches text pools across multiple ABAP programs and displays the matching entries using the SALV framework.

## Why RPR_ABAP_SOURCE_SCAN Was Not Enough

The standard report **RPR_ABAP_SOURCE_SCAN** is useful for searching ABAP source code. However, it does not search **Program Text Elements (Text Symbols)** because these texts are maintained in the program's text pool rather than in the source code.

During a maintenance activity, I needed to locate a company name maintained as a Program Text Element (Text Symbol).Since the standard report did not return these occurrences, I needed another approach to search the text pool directly.

To address this limitation, I developed a utility that reads the text pool of each selected program, searches for matching text entries, and displays the results using SALV.

Implementation

To address this limitation, I developed a lightweight utility that reads the text pool of each selected ABAP program, searches for matching text entries, and displays the results using SALV.

The implementation consists of the following steps:

  1. Retrieve the selected ABAP programs from the repository.
  2. Read the text pool of each program using READ TEXTPOOL.
  3. Search the retrieved text entries for the specified text.
  4. Display the matching results using SALV.

Step 1: Retrieve the Selected Programs

The first step is to retrieve the selected ABAP programs from the repository based on the user input provided on the selection screen.

SELECT pgmid,
object,
obj_name
FROM tadir
INTO TABLE @MT_programs
WHERE pgmid = 'R3TR'
AND obj_name IN @SO_prog.

The utility reads the selected repository objects from the TADIR table. Each program is then processed individually to search its Program Text Elements.

Step 2: Read the Program Text Pool

For each selected program, the utility uses the READ TEXTPOOL statement to retrieve the program's Text Elements for the selected language.

LOOP AT mt_programs INTO DATA(ls_program).

CLEAR mt_textpool.

READ TEXTPOOL ls_program-obj_name
LANGUAGE so_lang-low
INTO mt_textpool
STATE 'A'.

ENDLOOP.

READ TEXTPOOL retrieves the text pool for the current program. The selected language (so_lang-low) determines which language version is loaded into the internal table mt_textpool. These entries are then processed in the next step.

Step 3: Search for Matching Text

After reading the text pool, the utility loops through the retrieved entries and searches for the text entered on the selection screen. A partial text search is performed using the CS (Contains String) operator.

LOOP AT mt_textpool INTO DATA(ls_textpool)
WHERE id = 'I'
AND entry CS so_text-low.

APPEND VALUE ty_result(
program = ls_program-obj_name
key = ls_textpool-key
text = ls_textpool-entry )
TO mt_result.

ENDLOOP.

The CS operator performs a substring search, allowing partial matches instead of requiring an exact text match. Whenever a matching entry is found, the program name, text key, and text are added to the result table for later display.

Step 4: Display the Search Results

After all matching text entries have been collected, the results are displayed using the SAP List Viewer (SALV), providing a simple and interactive ALV report with standard features such as sorting, filtering, and exporting.

cl_salv_table=>factory(
IMPORTING
r_salv_table = DATA(lo_alv)
CHANGING
t_table = mt_result ).

lo_alv->get_functions( )->set_all( abap_true ).
lo_alv->display( ).

The result table is passed to the SALV factory method to create the ALV output. Standard SALV functions are enabled before displaying the results.

 

Selection Screen

Sample Output

 

SOURCE CODE

The complete source code is available on GitHub:

https://github.com/aravindhanchandran/abap-text-symbol-search 

The repository includes the complete implementation and can be used as a reference or extended to meet additional search requirements.

Conclusion

The standard report `RPR_ABAP_SOURCE_SCAN` is effective for searching ABAP source code, but it does not search Program Text Elements maintained in a program's text pool. This utility complements the standard report by using `READ TEXTPOOL` to search Text Symbols across multiple ABAP programs.

I hope this utility is useful for developers performing impact analysis, maintenance activities, company renaming projects, or any task that requires searching Program Text Elements. If you know of a SAP standard alternative or have suggestions for improvements, I'd be happy to hear your feedback. Contributions and ideas are always welcome.

entry.title: Question regarding IAS Group Filtering and Authorization Mapping
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

We have a question regarding the runtime processing of group attributes in SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS).

In our landscape, users are authenticated by Microsoft Entra ID, and many users belong to a large number of Entra groups that are used for different enterprise applications.

For some SAP applications, authorization is evaluated locally after authentication. However, for other SAP applications, authorization is based on mapping Microsoft Entra groups to application roles or SAP role collections. In these scenarios, the groups received in the SAML assertion (or other supported token) determine the user's access within the application.

Our question is:

If Microsoft Entra ID sends a multi-valued groups claim containing many group memberships (for example, 50 groups), can SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) filter or transform this attribute on a per-application basis so that only the groups relevant to the target application are included in the outgoing SAML assertion?

For example:

  • Microsoft Entra user belongs to 50 groups.
  • Signavio requires only 2 of those groups.
  • SAP Build Work Zone requires a different subset.
  • Another SAP application requires yet another subset.

Can IAS be configured so that each application receives only its relevant groups, without exposing unrelated Entra groups?

We are asking this because we would like IAS to act as the central identity and authorization abstraction layer for SAP applications. Our objective is to:

  • Minimize the number of groups sent to each application.
  • Avoid exposing unrelated enterprise groups to applications.
  • Keep SAML assertions small and application-specific.
  • Centralize authorization mapping within IAS instead of requiring application-specific group filtering.

If IAS supports this capability, could you please advise which feature or configuration should be used (for example, application-specific attribute mappings, conditional transformations, or another recommended approach)?

entry.title: ABAP development tools for Visual Studio Code: Version 1.1 released
entry.content:
What happened since the last release?

First of all, thanks for the positive feedback regarding the 1.0.1 patch which added the much requested RFC + password support! Please keep providing feedback as it helps us to plan the next releases. A lot of users are asking us how they can provide feedback. We have recently added a new customer influence channel just for the VS Code extension. We recommend using that one.

Last month, ABAPConf 2026 took place in Mannheim. We want to highlight two talks:

  1. Tobias Hofmann provided public feedback regarding the extension and compiled a nice to-do list. I can report that a lot of these to-do items are already part of our internal to-do list. The talk can be found here.
  2. Anne Keller and I gave a talk on the overall ABAP IDE strategy. Of course, it touches the VS Code extension but also aims to provide a broader picture on how we want to support ABAP IDEs via a single technology stack in the future and shares some insights on how the ADT MCP server will evolve in the future. The talk can be found here.

 

What gets shipped with this release?

Usability improvements for system connection handling

After creating a new destination there was no visual feedback that the destination was created successfully. We added a notification pop-up with a button which directly adds it as a folder to your workspace.

We added a "Log on to Destination..." command. While it was possible to log on to a destination via a right-click menu in the explorer view there was no command available for it.

Now, you can easily log on to a destination using the keyboard via the new command.

A significant improvement of object type support

A lot of requested object types get enabled by this release. Here is the list of added object types:

Classic ABAP source code
  • Create and Manage Function Groups and Modules
  • Create and Manage ABAP Programs and Includes
  • Create and Manage Lock Objects
Data Dictionary
  • Create and Manage Database Tables
  • Create and Manage Structures
  • Create and Manage Type Groups
RESTful Application Programming Model
  • Creating Behavior Definition Extensions
  • Enabling more OData Binding Types in Service Binding
  • Create and Manage CDS Entity Buffers

New MCP tools

Unified diff tool for transports

The unified diff tool generates a git like diff for a given transport request. The tool enables token efficient analysis of the changes contained in a transport request. This enables AI agents to fulfill requests such as:

  • "Explain transport XYZ"
  • "Generate a description for transport XYZ documenting what got changed and why"
  • "How much impact does transport XYZ has on the system? How dangerous is this transport?"
Tools for ABAP Test Cockpit (ATC)

The ATC MCP tools give AI agents access to running the ATC checks for a given change. This enables AI agents to fulfill requests such as:

  • "Implement [XYZ]. Afterwards, run the ATC checks and fix the reported findings."
  • "Run ATC checks for object XYZ"

Additional UX improvements

We added a command which allows you to quickly open the SAP Help documentation.

From each occurrence of a CDS annotation in a CDS data definition or CDS metadata extension, you can now navigate to its CDS annotation definition. To navigate, press F3 on the relevant CDS annotation. This helps you to investigate and to understand the use of a CDS annotation.

For more information see the new "What's New" page.

 

Sidenote: first version of ABAP cleaner VS Code extension available

Lots of community members asked for ABAP cleaner support in VS Code. We are happy to announce that the first version of the ABAP cleaner extension is available, now!

 

What's next?

From now on, we will stop listing upcoming features directly in the release blogs. Instead we have cleaned up and refreshed our ABAP Cloud Roadmap page. The planned features are listed there.

Provide feedback and influence our product backlog!

We welcome your feedback! Feel free to use the comment section or create a customer influence request to let us know which features you are missing the most. Your input will help us prioritize the feature backlog for the next releases.

entry.title: Join the SAP Distributed Energy Resources Partner Collaboration Space
entry.content:
The energy transition is accelerating, giving rise to new business opportunities and challenges in the utility industry. These advancements in both operational technologies and market roles has made close ecosystem collaboration even more critical. As utilities, technology providers, and implementation partners work together to address the growing complexity of distributed energy resources, we believe that open communication and knowledge sharing are essential to delivering innovative and scalable solutions.

To support this collaboration, SAP is pleased to introduce the SAP Distributed Energy Resources (SAP DER) Partner Zone, a dedicated space designed to keep our partner ecosystem informed, engaged, and connected.

 

Target Audience for SAP DER Partner Zone

The SAP DER Partner Zone is built specifically for our partner ecosystem. Whether you are developing complementary solutions, implementing customer projects, or exploring new opportunities within the DER ecosystem, this platform is here to help you stay informed, connected, and aligned with the latest developments across SAP DER solution.

 

Why We Built the SAP DER Partner Zone

The SAP DER Partner Zone has been created to provide a single destination where partners can access relevant information required to implement our SAP DER solution and actively engage with the SAP DER team.

More importantly, this is not intended to be a one-way communication channel. SAP DER Partner Zone is aimed to become a collaborative environment where ideas can be shared, questions can be raised, and valuable feedback can help shape the future of the SAP DER solution.

What You’ll Find in the SAP DER Partner Zone

As SAP DER continues to evolve, the Partner Zone will continue to grow with new content and resources. Today, you can access the following:

Collaboration Space: A timeline-based space where you can mention your questions, ideas, and feedback. This also includes your questions asked during the enablement sessions and responses from SAP.

Enablement Material: Recorded video and textual content on the following topics:

  • Overview of SAP DER, measurement concept management component solution
  • Integration of SAP DER, measurement concept management component with 3rd Party systems
  • Extension and customization of SAP DER, measurement concept management component solution
  • Configuration of SAP DER, measurement concept management component solution
  • Relevant Admin and user guides for SAP DER, measurement concept management component
  • Introductory videos of SAP DER, measurement concept management component and its data model
  • Several demos showing exciting features and capabilities of SAP DER, measurement concept management component

Supporting Documents: Links to relevant help pages, news and updates on SAP DER solution.

Development Documents: Available documents on API packages and API guides.

 

How to join SAP DER Partner Zone?

SAP DER Partner Zone is a private space therefore explicit registration is required to access the content.

To request the access, please follow the steps below:

  • Open the SAP DER Partner Zone link
  • Sign in using your SAP ID, select Register and complete the registration process.
  • Enter the required information on the next page to register for your SAP ID.
  • After signing in with your SAP ID, you will be directed to the SAP DER Partner Zone access page.
  • Just enter a short description for your access (Optional) and click on “Request to Join“.
  • Your request will be reviewed by the SAP DER team.
  • Once approved, you will gain access to the SAP DER Partner Zone and its resources. 

 

Moving Forward – Building the Future Together

The challenges and opportunities within distributed energy resources continue to evolve rapidly, and we believe that with close collaboration with our partners, we can build a strong and scalable solution ecosystem to ensure a smooth energy transition.

This Partner Zone is intended to become a central hub for that collaboration, a place where information flows in both directions. While we’ll use this platform to share updates and insights, we also encourage you to contribute your own perspectives, ask questions, and participate in discussions.

Your expertise, experience, and feedback are invaluable in helping us develop solutions that address critical customer needs and support the evolving energy landscape.

Thank you for being part of the SAP DER partner ecosystem. We look forward to working together and continuing to drive innovation in the utilities market.

entry.title: JSX Runtime for UI5: write your views as TSX, and tell us what you think
entry.content:
JSX Runtime for UI5: write your views as TSX, and tell us what you think

We've been experimenting with something and we'd like to put it in front of the community early: a JSX runtime for UI5. It lets you author UI5 views as .tsx files, with full per-control TypeScript typing, and it does so without a virtual DOM, and without a reconciler.

This is an incubation. It works, the showcase runs end-to-end, but the whole point of this post is to get it into your hands and hear back from you before we commit to more. If you write UI5 apps, we want your opinion.

What it actually does

A JSX element compiles to a plain constructor call. That's the entire idea:

import Button from "sap/m/Button";
import Page from "sap/m/Page";

export default class Main extends View {
    createContent() {
        return (
            <Page title="Hello">
                <Button text="Click me" press=".onPress" />
            </Page>
        );
    }
}

<Button text="Click me" /> becomes new Button({ text: "Click me" }). Nothing more magical than that. Babel emits the calls, the runtime hands them to UI5, and from there it's UI5 exactly as you already know it: metadata, applySettings, bindings, aggregations, events. There's no re-render loop, because a JSX expression is a constructor call, not a render description. Reactivity comes from UI5 bindings, same as in an XML view.

One thing to set expectations up front: this JSX describes the UI5 control tree, not the DOM. There's no native HTML. A <div> or <span> won't work, because a lowercase tag can't become a UI5 control. You compose controls (<Page>, <Button>, <VBox>, …), exactly as you do in an XML view, and UI5's renderers produce the actual markup. (An HTML-aware renderer is on the radar, but it's not here today.)

If you don't use TypeScript, plain .jsx works too. You still get the new Control(settings) calls, you just skip the author-time type checking.

Why we think it's worth a look

  • Per-control typing that we didn't hand-write. <Button> is typed from Button's own $ButtonSettings interface, which @openui5/types already publishes from each control's metadata. Wrong prop names, wrong value types, wrong event payloads. The TypeScript checker catches them. No code generation step in the project.
  • It's the control tree you already have. XML views and TSX views coexist in the same app. You can migrate one view at a time and keep everything runnable, or never migrate at all and just write new views in TSX.
  • No fork of UI5. The runtime doesn't patch or replace anything in the core. It glues Babel's output into UI5's existing settings model.
  • A small set of directives that read well. <Fragment> for grouping, <For> for bound aggregations, <If> for conditional rendering and falsy children ({flag && <X/>}) just work without crashing an aggregation.
  • A plugin SPI, if you want to extend it. Five extension points let libraries add their own directives and behaviors on top of the core without touching it. Everything the core itself does, the dot-handler events, <For>, <If>, is built on that same SPI, so nothing in the core is privileged. We ship a <Switch>/<Case>/<Default> sample plugin as a worked example.

Where to start

The fastest way to get a feel for it is the live showcase. It's a running UI5 app that doubles as the documentation, with runnable samples for every concept:

👉https://ui5-community.github.io/jsx-runtime/

The source, the setup guide, and the monorepo (the library, the showcase, and a minimal from-scratch reference app) are on GitHub:

👉https://github.com/ui5-community/jsx-runtime

If you already have an app with XML views, there's a step-by-step guide for converting them one at a time.

This is where you come in

We're calling this an incubation on purpose. Before we invest further, we want to know whether it solves a real problem for you, where it feels awkward, and what's missing. Concretely, we'd love to hear:

  • Would you write new views in TSX? Would you migrate existing ones?
  • Does the typing carry its weight, or is it more ceremony than value?
  • What breaks, or what did you expect to work and it didn't?
  • What would you need before you'd use this in a real project?

Please open an issue with anything: a bug, a rough edge, a "this is great," a "this is not for me and here's why":

👉https://github.com/ui5-community/jsx-runtime/issues

Honest feedback, including the critical kind, is exactly what makes an incubation worth doing. Thanks for taking a look.

entry.title: Re: Crystal Reports 2025 patch 1200 hangs during install
entry.content:
Often your AV and Firewall can block the installer because it is modifying your PC, you may need to temporarily disable them to get past the pause, there may be a pop-up waiting behind the progress you may not see or possibly on your second screen if you have one or it could be you don't have a second screen and the message has been sent to a non-second screen.

Hold the Shift and then hit the TAB key, and see if there are any screens showing permissions or some error etc. to possibly catch the error causing the pause.

Look in your AV logs to see if it blocked anything.

To remove upgrading issue using MSIExec.exe SAP Install team did the following:

1. Upgrade completely removes the last install, removing registry keys etc. before updating the new version

2. YOU MUST right click on the EXE and select Run as Administrator:

This gives the installer full permissions to remove/add the various dll's and registry entries.

Look here: C:\Users\YourUser name\AppData\Local\Temp\2026.07.21.05.30.58, date may be different

Open the latest install log, you should find a file called: setupengine.log

If there is one see if any errors are logged.

You may need to be patient, it can take a long time when upgrading

Great article on MCP Servers with SAP Integration Suite! I successfully deployed my MCP Server following your steps.

One question: in the section "Invoke the MCP Server Using an AI Client", you mention using Cline with PIR credentials but say the detailed steps are outside the scope of the blog.

Could you share how you configured Cline to connect to the MCP Server? I'm getting a Language model unavailable error and I'm not sure if it's related to the LLM provider configuration or the MCP authentication.

entry.title: Re: Vendor is not selected based on the Fixed vendor chec...
entry.content:
Hello @Pavan30 

As per note 3327584 - FAQ MRP Sourcing Guidance, 2.2.1 Fixed Vendor is not considered:

Within Source list it is possible to maintain a 'Fixed Vendor'. Idea is that within Procurement process this Vendor should be preferred, for example using transaction ME51n and using 'Source Determination' or button 'Assign Source of supply'. This flag and functionality is within MM-PUR but not part of PP-MRP.

...

To prefer the vendor, the source list flag 'Fix' (Fixed Source of Supply) was added as a SORT criteria for MRP, in case multiple sources are marked as relevant in source list (which is against SAP recommendations). Details are in SAP Note 2614920 Variant 2.

As per note 2614920 - Sourcing priority during MRP in S/4 HANA system, variant 2:

The following explanation shows step-by-step how system decides which source of supply should be picked.

  1. Source of supply assigned in quota.
  2. Source of supply for which a source list entry is maintained with MRP Planning <> ‘<space>’

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Re: Route Determination for One-Time Customers in S/4HANA...
entry.content:
@Manish_12 If I get it correctly, you get sales order data from an external source without transportation zones. Then you enhance the data and calculate transportation zones based on ZIP codes. Then you create sales orders with calculated transportation zones.

You say that sales order creation doesn't determine routes with the transportation zones provided. But when you change sales orders and change transportation zones, then routes are determined correctly.

If my understanding is correct, then it is a bug in S/4 and needs to be reported to SAP support. Sales order logic should determine a route with the transportation zone provided during creation as well as during change.

entry.title: Find your next SAP Enterprise Support Academy live session
entry.content:
Whether you're looking for a quick expert webcast or a hands-on implementation workshop, the new Schedules for Upcoming Live Sessions page provides a single view of upcoming SAP Enterprise Support Academy learning opportunities. With more than 300 live sessions currently available for enrollment, you can easily browse upcoming sessions and filter them by session type, language, start date, and time zone:

Choose from two live learning formats:

  • Meet-the-Expert (MTE) – Live webcasts covering a range of topics and the support aspects of the latest SAP technologies. Learn directly from SAP experts and ask your questions during the live Q&A. Most one-time sessions are also available as recordings.
  • Expert-guided Implementations (EGIs) – Hands-on, multi-day remote workshops designed to help you apply what you learn directly in your own SAP environment. Each session follows a simple, practical approach: an SAP expert explains each activity step by step, you complete the activity in your own system, and guidance is available throughout.

Looking for sessions in your preferred language? In addition to English, you can find live sessions in Spanish, Portuguese, Chinese, and Japanese. Simply use the Language filter to discover the sessions that best suit you, including a growing selection of Spanish-language Expert-guided Implementations.

Session topics cover a broad range of SAP solutions and technologies, helping you build skills, adopt best practices, and achieve your business goals.

All live sessions are included with SAP Enterprise Support and SAP Enterprise Support, cloud editions as part of the Foundational Success Plan. To register, sign in to the SAP Learning site with your S-User and access SAP Enterprise Support Academy.

👉 Browse the new scheduling overview.

Still deciding which session to join? Subscribe to our monthly newsletter for curated highlights, news, and the latest learning and enablement opportunities from SAP Enterprise Support Academy.

entry.title: Explore SAP Service Cloud Version 2 APIs
entry.content:
In my work to explore how to build AI Agents, finding and understanding APIs used in SAP Service Cloud Version 2, is a large part of the work. While using 'inspection' in the browser is a great tool I wanted to have a better option without creating a larger application.

When I asked Claude, it suggested to work with 'HAR' files that can be downloaded from the browser. I then asked Claude to create a viewer of this file and organize the information in a more readable form.

The result was the below view:

The workflow is super simple. Let's say we want to analyze what API calls are made when opening the Contract Account View in SAP Service Cloud Version 2.

  1. Open the browser in inspection mode

  2. Navigate to a step just before opening the Contract Account View

  3. Clear the network tab for any calls made so far

  4. Open the Contract Account in SAP Service Cloud Version 2

  5. Save the HAR file (see screenshot for steps in the readme on GitHub)

After that just open the html viewer. At the top there is a 'choose file' option, and select the file you saved above. That is, it.

On the view you see the list of calls and the sequence of the calls.

If you select one of the calls it will show the payload for the call:

 

After a few iterations with Claude, we added a few more features to the viewer that could be helpful for other things:

  1. The original target was to get a list of the API calls made

  2. In addition, the payloads are added for the call. This makes it easy to view the actual data.

  3. At the left top you can select calls that fail and look at the payload for the error. In the attached example where we have an error - the payload directly gives you this information: "message": "No active communication configuration found.". That is pretty helpful, now I know to go an activate that.

 

I have uploaded the viewer on GitHub, Link to viewer.

To download the viewer: open the link above, click the Raw button, then use File > Save As in your browser to save the file as viewer.html. Save it anywhere convenient — your Desktop, a project folder, wherever you keep your HAR files. SINCE THIS IS CODE DELIVERED 'AS-IS', PLEASE USE AT YOUR OWN RISK AND VALIDATE BEFORE EXECUTING.

 

⚠ Important Notice Before You Start

HAR files contain sensitive data. When you export a HAR file from your browser, it captures everything — full request and response bodies, session cookies, authentication tokens, and any personal or business data returned by the APIs. Treat HAR files as confidential documents. Do not share them over unencrypted channels, do not attach them to emails or public tickets, and delete them when they are no longer needed.

JavaScript must be enabled. The viewer is an HTML file that runs JavaScript locally in your browser. Some corporate browser security policies restrict JavaScript execution for files opened from the local filesystem (file:// URLs). If the viewer opens but does not respond when you load a HAR file, your organization's security policy may be blocking it — check with your IT department before proceeding.

 

Maybe you can take this and add additional features that would make it even better for you.

Have a great week.

entry.title: Re: Internal Order planning in SAP S/4HANA with SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content: Yes, the IOs created in S/4HANA are real IOs. You can use status management of an IO to allow actual postings in S/4HANA to it. The status management of the IO in S/4HANA only influences the allowed transactions in S/4HANA.
